title: Auschwitz concentration camp

infobox.value: Mainly Jews, Poles, Romani, Soviet prisoners of war

[Adolf Burger](/wiki/Adolf_Burger "Adolf Burger"), [Edith Eger](/wiki/Edith_Eger "Edith Eger"), [Anne Frank](/wiki/Anne_Frank "Anne Frank"), [Viktor Frankl](/wiki/Viktor_Frankl "Viktor Frankl"), [Imre Kertész](/wiki/Imre_Kert%C3%A9sz "Imre Kertész"), [Maximilian Kolbe](/wiki/Maximilian_Kolbe "Maximilian Kolbe"), [Primo Levi](/wiki/Primo_Levi "Primo Levi"), [Fritz Löhner-Beda](/wiki/Fritz_L%C3%B6hner-Beda "Fritz Löhner-Beda"), [Irène Némirovsky](/wiki/Ir%C3%A8ne_N%C3%A9mirovsky "Irène Némirovsky"), [Tadeusz Pietrzykowski](/wiki/Tadeusz_Pietrzykowski "Tadeusz Pietrzykowski"), [Witold Pilecki](/wiki/Witold_Pilecki "Witold Pilecki"), [Liliana Segre](/wiki/Liliana_Segre "Liliana Segre"), [Edith Stein](/wiki/Edith_Stein "Edith Stein"), [Simone Veil](/wiki/Simone_Veil "Simone Veil"), [Rudolf Vrba](/wiki/Rudolf_Vrba "Rudolf Vrba"), [Alfréd Wetzler](/wiki/Alfr%C3%A9d_Wetzler "Alfréd Wetzler"), [Elie Wiesel](/wiki/Elie_Wiesel "Elie Wiesel"), [Else Ury](/wiki/Else_Ury "Else Ury"), [Eddie Jaku](/wiki/Eddie_Jaku "Eddie Jaku"), [Władysław Bartoszewski](/wiki/W%C5%82adys%C5%82aw_Bartoszewski "Władysław Bartoszewski")

Auschwitz Birkenau, German Nazi Concentration and Extermination Camp (1940–1945)

(German: [[ˈaʊ̯ʃvɪts]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German")), also known as **Oświęcim** (Polish: [[ɔˈɕfjɛɲ.t͡ɕim]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish")),[[3]](#cite_note-3) was a complex of over 40 [concentration](/wiki/Nazi_concentration_camps "Nazi concentration camps") and [extermination camps](/wiki/Extermination_camp "Extermination camp") operated by [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") in [occupied Poland](/wiki/Polish_areas_annexed_by_Nazi_Germany "Polish areas annexed by Nazi Germany") (in a portion annexed into Germany in 1939)[[4]](#cite_note-4) during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust"). It consisted of [Auschwitz I](#Auschwitz_I), the main camp (*Stammlager*) in [Oświęcim](/wiki/O%C5%9Bwi%C4%99cim "Oświęcim"); [Auschwitz II-Birkenau](#Auschwitz_II-Birkenau), a concentration and extermination camp with [gas chambers](/wiki/Gas_chamber "Gas chamber"), [Auschwitz III-Monowitz](#Auschwitz_III), a [labour camp](/wiki/Arbeitslager "Arbeitslager") for the chemical conglomerate [IG Farben](/wiki/IG_Farben "IG Farben"), and [dozens of subcamps](/wiki/List_of_subcamps_of_Auschwitz "List of subcamps of Auschwitz").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Nazi concentration camp in Poland (1940–1945)

"Auschwitz" redirects here. For the town where the camp is located, see [Oświęcim](/wiki/O%C5%9Bwi%C4%99cim "Oświęcim"). For other uses, see [Auschwitz (disambiguation)](/wiki/Auschwitz_(disambiguation) "Auschwitz (disambiguation)").

**Auschwitz** (German: [[ˈaʊ̯ʃvɪts]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German")), also known as **Oświęcim** (Polish: [[ɔˈɕfjɛɲ.t͡ɕim]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish")),[[3]](#cite_note-3) was a complex of over 40 [concentration](/wiki/Nazi_concentration_camps "Nazi concentration camps") and [extermination camps](/wiki/Extermination_camp "Extermination camp") operated by [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") in [occupied Poland](/wiki/Polish_areas_annexed_by_Nazi_Germany "Polish areas annexed by Nazi Germany") (in a portion annexed into Germany in 1939)[[4]](#cite_note-4) during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust"). It consisted of [Auschwitz I](#Auschwitz_I), the main camp (*Stammlager*) in [Oświęcim](/wiki/O%C5%9Bwi%C4%99cim "Oświęcim"); [Auschwitz II-Birkenau](#Auschwitz_II-Birkenau), a concentration and extermination camp with [gas chambers](/wiki/Gas_chamber "Gas chamber"), [Auschwitz III-Monowitz](#Auschwitz_III), a [labour camp](/wiki/Arbeitslager "Arbeitslager") for the chemical conglomerate [IG Farben](/wiki/IG_Farben "IG Farben"), and [dozens of subcamps](/wiki/List_of_subcamps_of_Auschwitz "List of subcamps of Auschwitz").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

After Germany [initiated World War II](/wiki/Causes_of_World_War_II#Invasion_of_Poland "Causes of World War II") by [invading Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland") in September 1939, the *[Schutzstaffel](/wiki/Schutzstaffel "Schutzstaffel")* (SS) converted Auschwitz I, an army barracks, into a [prisoner-of-war camp](/wiki/Prisoner-of-war_camp "Prisoner-of-war camp").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDworkvan_Pelt2002166-6) The initial transport of [political detainees](/wiki/Holocaust_victims#Political_victims "Holocaust victims") to Auschwitz consisted almost solely of Poles (for whom the camp was initially established). For the first two years, the majority of inmates were Polish.[[7]](#cite_note-7) In May 1940, German criminals brought to the camp as [functionaries](#functionaries) established the camp's reputation for sadism. Prisoners were beaten, tortured, and executed for the most trivial of reasons. The first [gassings](/wiki/Extermination_camp#Gassings "Extermination camp")—of Soviet and Polish prisoners—took place in [block 11](#block_11) of Auschwitz I around August 1941.

Construction of Auschwitz II began the following month, and from 1942 until late 1944 freight trains delivered Jews from all over [German-occupied Europe](/wiki/German-occupied_Europe "German-occupied Europe") to its gas chambers. Of the [1.3 million people](#Death_toll) sent to Auschwitz, 1.1 million were murdered. The number of victims includes 960,000 Jews (865,000 of whom were gassed on arrival), 74,000 [non-Jewish Poles](/wiki/German_AB-Aktion_in_Poland "German AB-Aktion in Poland"), 21,000 [Romani](/wiki/Romani_Holocaust "Romani Holocaust"), 15,000 [Soviet prisoners of war](/wiki/German_atrocities_committed_against_Soviet_prisoners_of_war "German atrocities committed against Soviet prisoners of war"), and up to 15,000 others.[[8]](#cite_note-Piperfigures-8) Those not gassed were murdered via starvation, exhaustion, disease, individual executions, or beatings. Others were killed during [medical experiments](/wiki/Nazi_human_experimentation "Nazi human experimentation").

At least 802 prisoners tried to escape, 144 successfully, and on [7 October 1944](/wiki/Sonderkommando_Revolt_in_Auschwitz "Sonderkommando Revolt in Auschwitz"), two *[Sonderkommando](/wiki/Sonderkommando "Sonderkommando")* units, consisting of prisoners who operated the gas chambers, launched an unsuccessful uprising. After the Holocaust ended, only 789 *[Schutzstaffel](/wiki/Schutzstaffel "Schutzstaffel")* personnel (no more than 15 percent) ever stood trial.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik2000b116,_n. 19-9) Several were executed, including camp commandant [Rudolf Höss](/wiki/Rudolf_H%C3%B6ss "Rudolf Höss"). The [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II")' failure to act on early reports of mass murder by bombing the camp or its railways remains controversial.

As the Soviet [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") approached Auschwitz in January 1945, toward the end of the war, the SS sent most of the camp's population west on a [death march](/wiki/Death_marches_(Holocaust) "Death marches (Holocaust)") to camps inside Germany and Austria. Soviet troops [liberated the camp](#Liberation) on 27 January 1945, a day commemorated since 2005 as [International Holocaust Remembrance Day](/wiki/International_Holocaust_Remembrance_Day "International Holocaust Remembrance Day"). In the decades after the war, [survivors](/wiki/Holocaust_survivors "Holocaust survivors") such as [Primo Levi](/wiki/Primo_Levi "Primo Levi"), [Viktor Frankl](/wiki/Viktor_Frankl "Viktor Frankl"), [Elie Wiesel](/wiki/Elie_Wiesel "Elie Wiesel"), and [Edith Eger](/wiki/Edith_Eger "Edith Eger") wrote memoirs of their experiences, and the camp became a dominant symbol of the Holocaust. In 1947, Poland founded the [Auschwitz-Birkenau State Museum](/wiki/Auschwitz-Birkenau_State_Museum "Auschwitz-Birkenau State Museum") on the site of Auschwitz I and II, and in 1979 it was named a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO"). Auschwitz is the site of the largest mass murder in a single location in history.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Background

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/WW2-Holocaust-Europe.png/250px-WW2-Holocaust-Europe.png)](/wiki/File:WW2-Holocaust-Europe.png)

Camps and ghettos in [German-occupied Europe](/wiki/German-occupied_Europe "German-occupied Europe"), 1944

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Map_of_Auschwitz_and_environs%2C_1944.jpg/250px-Map_of_Auschwitz_and_environs%2C_1944.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Auschwitz_and_environs,_1944.jpg)

Auschwitz I, II, and III

The ideology of [Nazism](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism") combined elements of "[racial hygiene](/wiki/Racial_hygiene "Racial hygiene")", [eugenics](/wiki/Eugenics "Eugenics"), [antisemitism](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism"), [pan-Germanism](/wiki/Pan-Germanism "Pan-Germanism"), and territorial expansionism, [Richard J. Evans](/wiki/Richard_J._Evans "Richard J. Evans") writes.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans20057-12) [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") and his [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party") became obsessed by the "[Jewish question](/wiki/Jewish_question "Jewish question")".[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrowning2004424-13) Both during and immediately after the [Nazi seizure of power](/wiki/Nazi_seizure_of_power "Nazi seizure of power") in Germany in 1933, acts of violence against [German Jews](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Germany "History of the Jews in Germany") became ubiquitous,[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich201032–35,_41-14) and legislation was passed excluding them from certain professions, including the civil service and the law.[[a]](#cite_note-16)

Harassment and economic pressure [encouraged Jews to leave Germany](/wiki/Emigration_of_Jews_from_Nazi_Germany_and_German-occupied_Europe "Emigration of Jews from Nazi Germany and German-occupied Europe"); their businesses were denied access to markets, forbidden from advertising in newspapers, and deprived of government contracts.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich201041,_67–69-17) On 15 September 1935, the [Reichstag](/wiki/Reichstag_(Nazi_Germany) "Reichstag (Nazi Germany)") passed the [Nuremberg Laws](/wiki/Nuremberg_Laws "Nuremberg Laws"). One, the [Reich Citizenship Law](/wiki/Reich_Citizenship_Law "Reich Citizenship Law"), defined as citizens those of "German or related blood who demonstrate by their behaviour that they are willing and suitable to serve the German People and Reich faithfully", and the [Law for the Protection of German Blood and German Honor](/wiki/Law_for_the_Protection_of_German_Blood_and_German_Honor "Law for the Protection of German Blood and German Honor") prohibited marriage and extramarital relations between those with "German or related blood" and Jews.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich201060-18)

When Germany [invaded Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland") in September 1939, triggering World War II, Hitler ordered that the Polish leadership and intelligentsia be destroyed.[[18]](#cite_note-19) The area around Auschwitz was [annexed to the German Reich](/wiki/Polish_areas_annexed_by_Nazi_Germany "Polish areas annexed by Nazi Germany"), as part of first [Gau Silesia](/wiki/Gau_Silesia "Gau Silesia") and from 1941 [Gau Upper Silesia](/wiki/Gau_Upper_Silesia "Gau Upper Silesia").[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaar200941–46-20) The camp at Auschwitz was established in April 1940, at first as a quarantine camp for Polish political prisoners. On 22 June 1941, in an attempt to obtain new territory, Hitler [invaded the Soviet Union](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa").[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECesarani2016xxxiii-21) The first gassing at Auschwitz—of a group of Soviet prisoners of war—took place around August 1941.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b117-22) By the end of that year, during what most historians regard as the first phase of the Holocaust, 500,000–800,000 Soviet Jews had been murdered in mass shootings by a combination of German *[Einsatzgruppen](/wiki/Einsatzgruppen "Einsatzgruppen")*, ordinary German soldiers, and local collaborators.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatthäus2004244-23) At the [Wannsee Conference](/wiki/Wannsee_Conference "Wannsee Conference") in Berlin on 20 January 1942, [Reinhard Heydrich](/wiki/Reinhard_Heydrich "Reinhard Heydrich") outlined the [Final Solution to the Jewish Question](/wiki/Final_Solution_to_the_Jewish_Question "Final Solution to the Jewish Question") to senior Nazis,[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGerlach201684–85-24) and from early 1942 freight trains delivered Jews from all over [occupied Europe](/wiki/Occupied_Europe "Occupied Europe") to German [extermination camps](#Extermination_camps) in Poland: Auschwitz, [Bełżec](/wiki/Belzec_extermination_camp "Belzec extermination camp"), [Chełmno](/wiki/Che%C5%82mno_extermination_camp "Chełmno extermination camp"), [Majdanek](/wiki/Majdanek_concentration_camp "Majdanek concentration camp"), [Sobibór](/wiki/Sobibor_extermination_camp "Sobibor extermination camp"), and [Treblinka](/wiki/Treblinka_extermination_camp "Treblinka extermination camp"). Most prisoners were gassed on arrival.[[24]](#cite_note-deathcamps-25)

## Camps

### Auschwitz I

#### Growth

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Auschwitz_I_-_Birkenau%2C_O%C5%9Bwi%C4%99cim%2C_Polonia_-_panoramio_%285%29.jpg/250px-Auschwitz_I_-_Birkenau%2C_O%C5%9Bwi%C4%99cim%2C_Polonia_-_panoramio_%285%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auschwitz_I_-_Birkenau,_O%C5%9Bwi%C4%99cim,_Polonia_-_panoramio_(5).jpg)

Auschwitz I, 2013 ([50°01′39″N 19°12′18″E﻿ / ﻿50.0275°N 19.2050°E﻿ / 50.0275; 19.2050﻿ (Auschwitz I)](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Auschwitz_concentration_camp&params=50.0275_N_19.205_E_region:PL-MA_type:landmark&title=Auschwitz+I))

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Museum_Auschwitz.jpg/250px-Museum_Auschwitz.jpg)](/wiki/File:Museum_Auschwitz.jpg)

Auschwitz I, 2009; the prisoner reception center of Auschwitz I became the visitor reception center of the [Auschwitz-Birkenau State Museum](/wiki/Auschwitz-Birkenau_State_Museum "Auschwitz-Birkenau State Museum").[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDworkvan_Pelt2002362-26)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Auschwitz_I_visitor_reception_centre%2C_July_2014_%28panoramio%29_cropped.jpg/250px-Auschwitz_I_visitor_reception_centre%2C_July_2014_%28panoramio%29_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auschwitz_I_visitor_reception_centre,_July_2014_(panoramio)_cropped.jpg)

Former prisoner reception center; the building on the far left with the row of chimneys was the camp kitchen.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/AerialAuschwitz1944.jpg/250px-AerialAuschwitz1944.jpg)](/wiki/File:AerialAuschwitz1944.jpg)

An aerial reconnaissance photograph of the Auschwitz concentration camp showing the Auschwitz I camp, 4 April 1944

A former World War I camp for transient workers and later a Polish army barracks, Auschwitz I was the main camp (*Stammlager*) and administrative headquarters of the camp complex. Fifty kilometres (31 mi) southwest of [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków"), the site was first suggested in February 1940 as a quarantine camp for Polish prisoners by [Arpad Wigand](/wiki/Arpad_Wigand "Arpad Wigand"), the inspector of the [Sicherheitspolizei](/wiki/Sicherheitspolizei "Sicherheitspolizei") (security police) and deputy of [Erich von dem Bach-Zelewski](/wiki/Erich_von_dem_Bach-Zelewski "Erich von dem Bach-Zelewski"), the [Higher SS and Police Leader](/wiki/SS_and_Police_Leader "SS and Police Leader") for Silesia. [Richard Glücks](/wiki/Richard_Gl%C3%BCcks "Richard Glücks"), head of the [Concentration Camps Inspectorate](/wiki/Concentration_Camps_Inspectorate "Concentration Camps Inspectorate"), sent [Walter Eisfeld](/wiki/Walter_Eisfeld "Walter Eisfeld"), former commandant of the [Sachsenhausen concentration camp](/wiki/Sachsenhausen_concentration_camp "Sachsenhausen concentration camp") in [Oranienburg](/wiki/Oranienburg "Oranienburg"), Germany, to inspect it.[[26]](#cite_note-27) Around 1,000 metres (3,300 ft) long and 400 metres (1,300 ft) wide,[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGutman199816-28) Auschwitz consisted at the time of 22 brick buildings, eight of them two-story. A second story was added to the others in 1943 and eight new blocks were built.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

[Reichsführer-SS](/wiki/Reichsf%C3%BChrer-SS "Reichsführer-SS") [Heinrich Himmler](/wiki/Heinrich_Himmler "Heinrich Himmler"), head of the [SS](/wiki/Schutzstaffel "Schutzstaffel"), approved the site in April 1940 on the recommendation of SS-[Obersturmbannführer](/wiki/Obersturmbannf%C3%BChrer "Obersturmbannführer") [Rudolf Höss](/wiki/Rudolf_H%C3%B6ss "Rudolf Höss") of the camps inspectorate. Höss oversaw the development of the camp and served as its first commandant. The first 30 prisoners arrived on 20 May 1940 from the Sachsenhausen camp. German "career criminals" (*Berufsverbrecher*), the men were known as "greens" (*Grünen*) after the [green triangles](#Triangles) on their prison clothing. Brought to the camp as functionaries, this group did much to establish the sadism of early camp life, which was directed particularly at Polish inmates, until the political prisoners took over their roles.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIwaszko2000a15-30) [Bruno Brodniewicz](/wiki/Bruno_Brodniewicz "Bruno Brodniewicz"), the first prisoner (who was given serial number 1), became *[Lagerälteste](/wiki/Lager%C3%A4lteste "Lagerälteste")* (camp elder). The others were given positions such as *[kapo](/wiki/Kapo_(concentration_camp) "Kapo (concentration camp)")* and block supervisor.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

#### First mass transport

Further information: [First mass transport to Auschwitz concentration camp](/wiki/First_mass_transport_to_Auschwitz_concentration_camp "First mass transport to Auschwitz concentration camp")

The first mass transport—of 728 Polish male political prisoners, including Catholic priests and Jews—arrived on 14 June 1940 from [Tarnów](/wiki/Tarn%C3%B3w "Tarnów"), Poland. They were given serial numbers 31 to 758.[[b]](#cite_note-33) In a letter on 12 July 1940, Höss told Glücks that the local population was "fanatically Polish, ready to undertake any sort of operation against the hated SS men".[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzeleckaSetkiewicz200071-34) By the end of 1940, the SS had confiscated land around the camp to create a 40-square-kilometer (15 sq mi) "zone of interest" (*Interessengebiet*) patrolled by the SS, Gestapo and local police.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzeleckaSetkiewicz200072–73-35) By March 1941, 10,900 were imprisoned in the camp, most of them Poles.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGutman199816-28)

An inmate's first encounter with Auschwitz, if they were registered and not sent straight to the gas chamber, was at the prisoner reception centre near the gate with the *Arbeit macht frei* sign, where they were tattooed, shaved, disinfected, and given a striped prison uniform. Built between 1942 and 1944, the center contained a bathhouse, laundry, and 19 gas chambers for delousing clothing. The prisoner reception center of Auschwitz I became the visitor reception center of the [Auschwitz-Birkenau State Museum](/wiki/Auschwitz-Birkenau_State_Museum "Auschwitz-Birkenau State Museum").[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDworkvan_Pelt2002362-26)

#### Crematorium I, first gassings

Further information: [§ Gas chambers](#Gas_chambers)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Auschwitz_I_krematorium.jpg/250px-Auschwitz_I_krematorium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auschwitz_I_krematorium.jpg)

Crematorium I, photographed in 2016, reconstructed after the war[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDworkvan_Pelt2002364-36)

Construction of crematorium I began at Auschwitz I at the end of June or beginning of July 1940.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b121-37) Initially intended not for mass murder but for prisoners who had been executed or had otherwise died in the camp, the crematorium was in operation from August 1940 until July 1943, by which time the crematoria at Auschwitz II had taken over.[[36]](#cite_note-38) By May 1942 three ovens had been installed in crematorium I, which together could burn 340 bodies in 24 hours.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b128-39)

The first experimental gassing took place around August 1941, when Lagerführer [Karl Fritzsch](/wiki/Karl_Fritzsch "Karl Fritzsch"), at the instruction of Rudolf Höss, murdered a group of Soviet prisoners of war by throwing [Zyklon B](/wiki/Zyklon_B "Zyklon B") crystals into their basement cell in [block 11](/wiki/Block_11 "Block 11") of Auschwitz I. A second group of 600 Soviet prisoners of war and around 250 sick Polish prisoners were gassed on 3–5 September in the old crematorium after being told they were to march naked there to receive new clothing.[[38]](#cite_note-40)[[39]](#cite_note-41) The morgue was later converted to a gas chamber able to hold at least 700–800 people.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b128-39)[[c]](#cite_note-42) Zyklon B was dropped into the room through slits in the ceiling.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b128-39)

#### First mass transport of Jews

Further information: [Bytom Synagogue](/wiki/Bytom_Synagogue "Bytom Synagogue") and [Beuthen Jewish Community](/wiki/Beuthen_Jewish_Community "Beuthen Jewish Community")

Historians have disagreed about the date the all-Jewish transports began arriving in Auschwitz. At the [Wannsee Conference](/wiki/Wannsee_Conference "Wannsee Conference") in Berlin on 20 January 1942, the Nazi leadership outlined, in euphemistic language, its plans for the [Final Solution](/wiki/Final_Solution "Final Solution").[[40]](#cite_note-43) According to [Franciszek Piper](/wiki/Franciszek_Piper "Franciszek Piper"), the Auschwitz commandant Rudolf Höss offered inconsistent accounts after the war, suggesting the extermination began in December 1941, January 1942, or before the establishment of the women's camp in March 1942.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000a61-44) In *Kommandant in Auschwitz*, he wrote: "In the spring of 1942 the first transports of Jews, all earmarked for extermination, arrived from Upper Silesia."[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHöss2003148-45) On 15 February 1942, according to [Danuta Czech](/wiki/Danuta_Czech "Danuta Czech"), a transport of Jews from Beuthen, [Upper Silesia](/wiki/Upper_Silesia "Upper Silesia") ([Bytom](/wiki/Bytom "Bytom"), Poland), arrived at Auschwitz I and was sent straight to the gas chamber.[[d]](#cite_note-47)[[44]](#cite_note-48) In 1998 an eyewitness said the train contained "the women of Beuthen".[[e]](#cite_note-50) [Saul Friedländer](/wiki/Saul_Friedl%C3%A4nder "Saul Friedländer") wrote that the Beuthen Jews were from the [Organization Schmelt](/wiki/Organization_Schmelt "Organization Schmelt") labor camps and had been deemed unfit for work.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriedländer2007359-51) According to [Christopher Browning](/wiki/Christopher_Browning "Christopher Browning"), transports of Jews unfit for work were sent to the gas chamber at Auschwitz from autumn 1941.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrowning2004357-52) The evidence for this and the February 1942 transport was contested in 2015 by [Nikolaus Wachsmann](/wiki/Nikolaus_Wachsmann "Nikolaus Wachsmann").[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWachsmann2015707-53)

Around 20 March 1942, according to Danuta Czech, a transport of Polish Jews from [Silesia](/wiki/Silesia "Silesia") and [Zagłębie Dąbrowskie](/wiki/D%C4%85browa_Basin "Dąbrowa Basin") was taken straight from the station to the Auschwitz II gas chamber, which had just come into operation.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000143-54) On 26 and 28 March, two transports of Slovakian Jews were registered as prisoners in the [women's camp](#Women's_camp), where they were kept for slave labour; these were the first transports organized by [Adolf Eichmann](/wiki/Adolf_Eichmann "Adolf Eichmann")'s [department IV B4](/wiki/Reich_Security_Head_Office_Referat_IV_B4 "Reich Security Head Office Referat IV B4") (the Jewish office) in the [Reich Security Head Office](/wiki/Reich_Security_Head_Office "Reich Security Head Office") (RSHA).[[f]](#cite_note-56) On 30 March the first RHSA transport arrived from France.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000144-55) "Selection", where new arrivals were chosen for work or the gas chamber, began in April 1942 and was conducted regularly from July. Piper writes that this reflected Germany's increasing need for labour. Those selected as unfit for work were gassed without being registered as prisoners.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000a62-57)

There is also disagreement about how many were gassed in Auschwitz I. [Perry Broad](/wiki/Perry_Broad "Perry Broad"), an *SS-Unterscharführer*, wrote that "transport after transport vanished in the Auschwitz [I] crematorium."[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b133,_n. 419-58) In the view of [Filip Müller](/wiki/Filip_M%C3%BCller "Filip Müller"), one of the Auschwitz I *[Sonderkommando](/wiki/Sonderkommando "Sonderkommando")*, tens of thousands of Jews were murdered there from France, the Netherlands, Slovakia, Upper Silesia, and Yugoslavia, and from the [Theresienstadt](/wiki/Theresienstadt "Theresienstadt"), [Ciechanow](/wiki/Ciechanow "Ciechanow"), and [Grodno](/wiki/Grodno_Ghetto "Grodno Ghetto") ghettos.[[53]](#cite_note-Müller_1999_31-59) In contrast, [Jean-Claude Pressac](/wiki/Jean-Claude_Pressac "Jean-Claude Pressac") estimated that up to 10,000 people had been murdered in Auschwitz I.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b133,_n. 419-58) The last inmates gassed there, in December 1942, were around 400 members of the Auschwitz II *Sonderkommando*, who had been forced to dig up and burn the remains of that camp's mass graves, thought to hold over 100,000 corpses.[[54]](#cite_note-60)

### Auschwitz II-Birkenau

"Birkenau" redirects here. For other uses, see [Birkenau (disambiguation)](/wiki/Birkenau_(disambiguation) "Birkenau (disambiguation)").

#### Construction

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Selection_on_the_ramp_at_Auschwitz-Birkenau%2C_1944_%28Auschwitz_Album%29_1a.jpg/250px-Selection_on_the_ramp_at_Auschwitz-Birkenau%2C_1944_%28Auschwitz_Album%29_1a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Selection_on_the_ramp_at_Auschwitz-Birkenau,_1944_(Auschwitz_Album)_1a.jpg)

May/June 1944: "Selection" of [Hungarian Jews](/wiki/Holocaust_in_Hungary "Holocaust in Hungary") for work or the [gas chamber](/wiki/Gas_chamber "Gas chamber"). From the [Auschwitz Album](/wiki/Auschwitz_Album "Auschwitz Album"), taken by the camp's [Erkennungsdienst](/wiki/Auschwitz_Erkennungsdienst "Auschwitz Erkennungsdienst").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Museum_Auschwitz_Birkenau.jpg/250px-Museum_Auschwitz_Birkenau.jpg)](/wiki/File:Museum_Auschwitz_Birkenau.jpg)

Gate with the camp remains in the background, 2009

After visiting Auschwitz I in March 1941, it appears that Himmler ordered that the camp be expanded,[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b144-61) although [Peter Hayes](/wiki/Peter_Hayes_(historian) "Peter Hayes (historian)") notes that, on 10 January 1941, the Polish underground told the [Polish government-in-exile](/wiki/Polish_government-in-exile "Polish government-in-exile") in London: "the Auschwitz concentration camp ...can accommodate approximately 7,000 prisoners at present, and is to be rebuilt to hold approximately 30,000."[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayes2003335-62) Construction of Auschwitz II-Birkenau—called a *Kriegsgefangenenlager* (prisoner-of-war camp) on blueprints—began in October 1941 in [Brzezinka](/wiki/Brzezinka "Brzezinka"), about three kilometers from Auschwitz I.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b144,_155_for_''Kriegsgefangenenlager''-63) The initial plan was that Auschwitz II would consist of four sectors (Bauabschnitte I–IV), each consisting of six subcamps (BIIa–BIIf) with their own gates and fences. The first two sectors were completed (sector BI was initially a quarantine camp), but the construction of BIII began in 1943 and stopped in April 1944, and the plan for BIV was abandoned.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzeleckaSetkiewicz200080–83-64)

SS-Sturmbannführer [Karl Bischoff](/wiki/Karl_Bischoff "Karl Bischoff"), an architect, was the chief of construction.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b144-61) Based on an initial budget of [RM](/wiki/Reichsmark "Reichsmark") 8.9 million, his plans called for each barracks to hold 550 prisoners, but he later changed this to 744 per barracks, which meant the camp could hold 125,000, rather than 97,000.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_Pelt1998118–119-65) There were 174 barracks, each measuring 35.4 by 11.0 m (116 by 36 ft), divided into 62 bays of 4 m2 (43 sq ft). The bays were divided into "roosts", initially for three inmates and later for four. With personal space of 1 m2 (11 sq ft) to sleep and place whatever belongings they had, inmates were deprived, [Robert-Jan van Pelt](/wiki/Robert-Jan_van_Pelt "Robert-Jan van Pelt") wrote, "of the minimum space needed to exist".[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_Pelt1998122–123-66)

The prisoners were forced to live in the barracks as they were building them; in addition to working, they faced long roll calls at night. As a result, most prisoners in BIb (the men's camp) in the early months died of [hypothermia](/wiki/Hypothermia "Hypothermia"), starvation or exhaustion within a few weeks.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzeleckaSetkiewicz200087-67) Some 10,000 Soviet prisoners of war arrived at Auschwitz I between 7 and 25 October 1941,[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000138–139-68) but by 1 March 1942 only 945 were still registered; they were transferred to Auschwitz II,[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000142-46) where most of them had died by May.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinbacher200594-69)

#### Crematoria II–V

Further information: [§ Gas chambers](#Gas_chambers)

The first gas chamber at Auschwitz II was operational by March 1942. On or around 20 March, a transport of Polish Jews sent by the Gestapo from [Silesia](/wiki/Silesia "Silesia") and [Zagłębie Dąbrowskie](/wiki/D%C4%85browa_Basin "Dąbrowa Basin") was taken straight from the [Oświęcim](/wiki/O%C5%9Bwi%C4%99cim "Oświęcim") freight station to the Auschwitz II gas chamber, then buried in a nearby meadow.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000143-54) The gas chamber was located in what prisoners called the "little red house" (known as bunker 1 by the SS), a brick cottage that had been turned into a gassing facility; the windows had been bricked up and its four rooms converted into two insulated rooms, the doors of which said "*Zur Desinfektion*" ("to disinfection"). A second brick cottage, the "little white house" or bunker 2, was converted and operational by June 1942.[[64]](#cite_note-70) When Himmler visited the camp on 17 and 18 July 1942, he was given a demonstration of a selection of Dutch Jews, a mass-murder in a gas chamber in bunker 2, and a tour of the building site of Auschwitz III, the new [IG Farben](/wiki/IG_Farben "IG Farben") plant being constructed at [Monowitz](/wiki/Monowitz "Monowitz").[[65]](#cite_note-71) Use of bunkers I and 2 stopped in Spring 1943 when the new crematoria were built, although bunker 2 became operational again in May 1944 for the murder of the Hungarian Jews. Bunker I was demolished in 1943 and bunker 2 in November 1944.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b143-72)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Auschwitz-crematorium-II-openings-1943.jpg/500px-Auschwitz-crematorium-II-openings-1943.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auschwitz-crematorium-II-openings-1943.jpg)

The openings through which Zyklon B was poured into the gas chamber of crematoria II are visible in this February 1943 photo, taken by SS Dietrich Kammann.[[67]](#cite_note-73) Poles Ludwik Lawin and Tadeusz Kubik, who worked in the camp photography studio, stole a number of Kammann's [negatives](/wiki/Negative_(photography) "Negative (photography)") and buried them.[[68]](#cite_note-74)

Plans for crematoria II and III show that both had an oven room 30 by 11.24 m (98.4 by 36.9 ft) on the ground floor, and an underground dressing room 49.43 by 7.93 m (162.2 by 26.0 ft) and gas chamber 30 by 7 m (98 by 23 ft). The dressing rooms had wooden benches along the walls and numbered pegs for clothing. Victims would be led from these rooms to a five-yard-long narrow corridor, which in turn led to a space from which the gas chamber door opened. The chambers were white inside, and nozzles were fixed to the ceiling to resemble showerheads.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b165–166-75) The daily capacity of the crematoria (how many bodies could be burned in a 24-hour period) was 340 corpses in crematorium I; 1,440 each in crematoria II and III; and 768 each in IV and V.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b159-76) By June 1943 all four crematoria were operational, but crematorium I was not used after July 1943. This made the total daily capacity 4,416, although by loading three to five corpses at a time, the *Sonderkommando* were able to burn some 8,000 bodies a day. This maximum capacity was rarely needed; the average between 1942 and 1944 was 1,000 bodies burned every day.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b164-77)

### Auschwitz III–Monowitz

Main article: [Monowitz concentration camp](/wiki/Monowitz_concentration_camp "Monowitz concentration camp")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/FARBEN_DWORY.png/250px-FARBEN_DWORY.png)](/wiki/File:FARBEN_DWORY.png)

Detailed map of [Buna Werke](/wiki/Monowitz_Buna_Werke "Monowitz Buna Werke"), [Monowitz](/wiki/Monowitz "Monowitz"), and nearby subcamps

After examining several sites for a new plant to manufacture [Buna-N](/wiki/Nitrile_rubber "Nitrile rubber"), a type of [synthetic rubber](/wiki/Synthetic_rubber "Synthetic rubber") essential to the war effort, the German chemical conglomerate [IG Farben](/wiki/IG_Farben "IG Farben") chose a site near the towns of [Dwory](/wiki/Dwory_II "Dwory II") and Monowice (Monowitz in German), about 7 km (4.3 mi) east of Auschwitz I.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinbacher200545-78) Tax exemptions were available to corporations prepared to develop industries in the frontier regions under the Eastern Fiscal Assistance Law, passed in December 1940. In addition to its proximity to the concentration camp, a source of cheap labour, the site had good railway connections and access to raw materials.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHilberg199881–82-79) In February 1941, Himmler ordered that the Jewish population of [Oświęcim](/wiki/O%C5%9Bwi%C4%99cim "Oświęcim") be expelled to make way for skilled laborers; that all Poles able to work remain in the town and work on building the factory; and that Auschwitz prisoners be used in the construction work.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinbacher200549-80)

Auschwitz inmates began working at the plant, known as Buna Werke and IG-Auschwitz, in April 1941, demolishing houses in Monowitz to make way for it.[[75]](#cite_note-81) By May, because of a shortage of trucks, several hundred of them were rising at 3 am to walk there twice a day from Auschwitz I.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzeleckaSetkiewicz2000108-82) Because a long line of exhausted inmates walking through the town of Oświęcim might harm German-Polish relations, the inmates were told to shave daily, make sure they were clean, and sing as they walked. From late July they were taken to the factory by train on freight wagons.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzeleckaSetkiewicz2000109–110-83) Given the difficulty of moving them, including during the winter, IG Farben decided to build a camp at the plant. The first inmates moved there on 30 October 1942.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzeleckaSetkiewicz2000111–112-84) Known as *KL Auschwitz III–Aussenlager* (Auschwitz III subcamp), and later as the Monowitz concentration camp,[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik2000a151–152-85) it was the first concentration camp to be financed and built by private industry.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinbacher200553-86)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Heinrich_Himmler%2C_IG_Farben_Auschwitz_plant%2C_July_1942.jpeg/250px-Heinrich_Himmler%2C_IG_Farben_Auschwitz_plant%2C_July_1942.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Heinrich_Himmler,_IG_Farben_Auschwitz_plant,_July_1942.jpeg)

[Heinrich Himmler](/wiki/Heinrich_Himmler "Heinrich Himmler") *(second left)* visits the [IG Farben](/wiki/IG_Farben "IG Farben") plant in Auschwitz III, July 1942.

Measuring 270 m × 490 m (890 ft × 1,610 ft), the camp was larger than Auschwitz I. By the end of 1944, it housed 60 barracks measuring 17.5 m × 8 m (57 ft × 26 ft), each with a day room and a sleeping room containing 56 three-tiered wooden bunks.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzeleckaSetkiewicz2000112-87) IG Farben paid the SS three or four [Reichsmark](/wiki/Reichsmark "Reichsmark") for nine- to eleven-hour shifts from each worker.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayes2001353-88) In 1943–1944, about 35,000 inmates worked at the plant; 23,000 (32 a day on average) were killed through malnutrition, disease, and the workload. Within three to four months at the camp, [Peter Hayes](/wiki/Peter_Hayes_(historian) "Peter Hayes (historian)") writes, the inmates were "reduced to walking skeletons".[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayes2001359-89) Deaths and transfers to the gas chambers at Auschwitz II reduced the population by nearly a fifth each month.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrakowski199857-90) Site managers constantly threatened inmates with the gas chambers, and the smell from the crematoria at Auschwitz I and II hung heavy over the camp.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayes2001364-91)

Although the factory had been expected to begin production in 1943, shortages of labour and raw materials meant start-up was postponed repeatedly.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinbacher200552,_56-92) The Allies bombed the plant in 1944 on 20 August, 13 September, 18 December, and 26 December. On 19 January 1945, the SS ordered that the site be evacuated, sending 9,000 inmates, most of them Jews, on a death march to another Auschwitz subcamp at [Gliwice](/wiki/Gliwice "Gliwice").[[87]](#cite_note-93) From Gliwice, prisoners were taken by rail in open freight wagons to the [Buchenwald](/wiki/Buchenwald "Buchenwald") and [Mauthausen](/wiki/Mauthausen-Gusen_concentration_camp_complex "Mauthausen-Gusen concentration camp complex") concentration camps. The 800 inmates who had been left behind in the Monowitz hospital were liberated along with the rest of the camp on 27 January 1945 by the [1st Ukrainian Front](/wiki/1st_Ukrainian_Front "1st Ukrainian Front") of the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army").[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzeleckaSetkiewicz2000115-94)

### Subcamps

Further information: [List of subcamps of Auschwitz](/wiki/List_of_subcamps_of_Auschwitz "List of subcamps of Auschwitz")

Several other German industrial enterprises, such as [Krupp](/wiki/Krupp "Krupp") and [Siemens-Schuckert](/wiki/Siemens-Schuckert "Siemens-Schuckert"), built factories with their own subcamps.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinbacher200557-95) There were around 28 camps near industrial plants, each camp holding hundreds or thousands of prisoners.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzeleckaSetkiewicz2000103–104-96) Designated as *Aussenlager* (external camp), *Nebenlager* (extension camp), *Arbeitslager* (labor camp), or *Aussenkommando* (external work detail),[[91]](#cite_note-97) camps were built at [Blechhammer](/wiki/Blechhammer "Blechhammer"), [Jawiszowice](/wiki/Jawiszowice "Jawiszowice"), [Jaworzno](/wiki/Central_Labour_Camp_Jaworzno "Central Labour Camp Jaworzno"), [Lagisze](/wiki/B%C4%99dzin "Będzin"), [Mysłowice](/wiki/Mys%C5%82owice "Mysłowice"), [Trzebinia](/wiki/Trzebinia "Trzebinia"), and as far afield as the [Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia](/wiki/Protectorate_of_Bohemia_and_Moravia "Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia") in Czechoslovakia.[[92]](#cite_note-98) Industries with satellite camps included coal mines, foundries and other metal works, and chemical plants. Prisoners were also made to work in forestry and farming.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGutman199817–18-99) For example, *Wirtschaftshof Budy*, in the Polish village of Budy near [Brzeszcze](/wiki/Brzeszcze "Brzeszcze"), was a farming subcamp where prisoners worked 12-hour days in the fields, tending animals, and making compost by mixing human ashes from the crematoria with sod and manure.[[94]](#cite_note-100) Incidents of sabotage to decrease production took place in several subcamps, including Charlottengrube, [Gleiwitz II](/wiki/Gliwice "Gliwice"), and [Rajsko](/wiki/Rajsko,_O%C5%9Bwi%C4%99cim_County "Rajsko, Oświęcim County").[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunin-Wasowicz1984139-101) Living conditions in some of the camps were so poor that they were regarded as punishment subcamps.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzeleckaSetkiewicz2000104-102)

## Life in the camps

### SS garrison

Main articles: [SS command of Auschwitz concentration camp](/wiki/SS_command_of_Auschwitz_concentration_camp "SS command of Auschwitz concentration camp") and [SS-Totenkopfverbände](/wiki/SS-Totenkopfverb%C3%A4nde "SS-Totenkopfverbände")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Richard_Baer%2C_Josef_Mengele%2C_Rudolf_Hoess%2C_Auschwitz._Album_H%C3%B6cker.jpg/250px-Richard_Baer%2C_Josef_Mengele%2C_Rudolf_Hoess%2C_Auschwitz._Album_H%C3%B6cker.jpg)](/wiki/File:Richard_Baer,_Josef_Mengele,_Rudolf_Hoess,_Auschwitz._Album_H%C3%B6cker.jpg)

From the [Höcker Album](/wiki/H%C3%B6cker_Album "Höcker Album") (*left to right*): [Richard Baer](/wiki/Richard_Baer "Richard Baer") (Auschwitz commandant from May 1944), [Josef Mengele](/wiki/Josef_Mengele "Josef Mengele") (camp physician), and [Rudolf Höss](/wiki/Rudolf_H%C3%B6ss "Rudolf Höss") (first commandant) in [Solahütte](/wiki/Solah%C3%BCtte "Solahütte"), an SS resort near Auschwitz, summer 1944.[[97]](#cite_note-Wilkinson17March2008-103)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/784648_O%C5%9Bwi%C4%99cim%2C_ob%C3%B3z_zag%C5%82ady_%22Auschwitz%22_-dom_komendanta_obozu_05.JPG/250px-784648_O%C5%9Bwi%C4%99cim%2C_ob%C3%B3z_zag%C5%82ady_%22Auschwitz%22_-dom_komendanta_obozu_05.JPG)](/wiki/File:784648_O%C5%9Bwi%C4%99cim,_ob%C3%B3z_zag%C5%82ady_%22Auschwitz%22_-dom_komendanta_obozu_05.JPG)

The commandant's and administration building, Auschwitz I

[Rudolf Höss](/wiki/Rudolf_H%C3%B6ss "Rudolf Höss"), born in [Baden-Baden](/wiki/Baden-Baden "Baden-Baden") in 1901,[[98]](#cite_note-104) was named the first commandant of Auschwitz when [Heinrich Himmler](/wiki/Heinrich_Himmler "Heinrich Himmler") ordered on 27 April 1940 that the camp be established.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik2000a154-105) Living with his wife and children in a two-story [stucco](/wiki/Stucco "Stucco") house near the commandant's and administration building,[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarding2013100-106) he served as commandant until 11 November 1943,[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik2000a154-105) with [Josef Kramer](/wiki/Josef_Kramer "Josef Kramer") as his deputy.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGutman199816-28) Succeeded as commandant by [Arthur Liebehenschel](/wiki/Arthur_Liebehenschel "Arthur Liebehenschel"),[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik2000a154-105) Höss joined the SS [Business and Administration Head Office](/wiki/SS_Main_Economic_and_Administrative_Office "SS Main Economic and Administrative Office") in Oranienburg as director of Amt DI,[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik2000a154-105) a post that made him deputy of the camps inspectorate.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik1998b294–295-107)

[Richard Baer](/wiki/Richard_Baer "Richard Baer") became commandant of Auschwitz I on 11 May 1944 and [Fritz Hartjenstein](/wiki/Fritz_Hartjenstein "Fritz Hartjenstein") of Auschwitz II from 22 November 1943, followed by Josef Kramer from 15 May 1944 until the camp's liquidation in January 1945. [Heinrich Schwarz](/wiki/Heinrich_Schwarz "Heinrich Schwarz") was commandant of Auschwitz III from the point at which it became an autonomous camp in November 1943 until its liquidation.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik2000a153–157-108) Höss returned to Auschwitz between 8 May and 29 July 1944 as the local SS garrison commander (*Standortältester*) to oversee the arrival of Hungary's Jews, which made him the superior officer of all the commandants of the Auschwitz camps.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik2000a154-105)

According to [Aleksander Lasik](/wiki/Aleksander_Lasik "Aleksander Lasik"), about 6,335 people (6,161 of them men) worked for the SS at Auschwitz over the course of the camp's existence;[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik2000b314-109) 4.2 percent were officers, 26.1 percent non-commissioned officers, and 69.7 percent rank and file.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik1998a282-110) In March 1941, there were 700 SS guards; in June 1942, 2,000; and in August 1944, 3,342. At its peak in January 1945, 4,480 SS men and 71 SS women worked in Auschwitz; the higher number is probably attributable to the logistics of evacuating the camp.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik2000b299-111) Female guards were known as SS supervisors (*SS-Aufseherinnen*).[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik1998a274-112)

Most of the staff were from Germany or Austria, but as the war progressed, increasing numbers of *[Volksdeutsche](/wiki/Volksdeutsche "Volksdeutsche")* from other countries, including Czechoslovakia, Poland, Yugoslavia, and the Baltic states, joined the SS at Auschwitz. Not all were ethnically German. Guards were also recruited from Hungary, Romania, and Slovakia.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik2000b323–324-113) Camp guards, around three quarters of the SS personnel, were members of the *[SS-Totenkopfverbände](/wiki/SS-Totenkopfverb%C3%A4nde "SS-Totenkopfverbände")* ([death's head](/wiki/Totenkopf "Totenkopf") units).[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik1998a273-114) Other SS staff worked in the medical or political departments, or in the economic administration, which was responsible for clothing and other supplies, including the property of dead prisoners.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik1998a272–273-115) The SS viewed Auschwitz as a comfortable posting; being there meant they had avoided the front and had access to the victims' property.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik1998a285-116)

### Functionaries and *Sonderkommando*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Bloki_Auschwitz.jpg/250px-Bloki_Auschwitz.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bloki_Auschwitz.jpg)

Auschwitz I, 2009

Certain prisoners, at first non-Jewish Germans but later Jews and non-Jewish Poles,[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000a49-117) were assigned positions of authority as *Funktionshäftlinge* (functionaries), which gave them access to better housing and food. The *Lagerprominenz* (camp elite) included *Blockschreiber* (barracks clerk), *[Kapo](/wiki/Kapo_(concentration_camp) "Kapo (concentration camp)")* (overseer), *Stubendienst* (barracks orderly), and *Kommandierte* (trusties).[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinbacher200535–36-118) Wielding tremendous power over other prisoners, the functionaries developed a reputation as sadists.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000a49-117) Very few were prosecuted after the war, because of the difficulty of determining which atrocities had been performed by order of the SS.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWittmann2003519–520-119)

Although the SS oversaw the murders at each gas chamber, the forced labor portion of the work was done by prisoners known from 1942 as the *[Sonderkommando](/wiki/Sonderkommando "Sonderkommando")* (special squad).[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b180-120) These were mostly Jews but they included groups such as Soviet POWs. In 1940–1941 when there was one gas chamber, there were 20 such prisoners, in late 1943 there were 400, and by 1944 during the Holocaust in Hungary the number had risen to 874.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b180–181,_184-121) The *Sonderkommando* removed goods and corpses from the incoming trains, guided victims to the dressing rooms and gas chambers, removed their bodies afterwards, and took their jewelry, hair, dental work, and any precious metals from their teeth, all of which was sent to Germany. Once the bodies were stripped of anything valuable, the *Sonderkommando* burned them in the crematoria.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b170–171-122)

Because they were witnesses to the mass murder, the *Sonderkommando* lived separately from the other prisoners, although this rule was not applied to the non-Jews among them.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b189-123) Their quality of life was further improved by their access to the property of new arrivals, which they traded within the camp, including with the SS.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b190–191-124) Nevertheless, their life expectancy was short; they were regularly murdered and replaced.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b180–181-125) About 100 survived to the camp's liquidation. They were forced on a death march and by train to the camp at [Mauthausen](/wiki/Mauthausen-Gusen_concentration_camp_complex "Mauthausen-Gusen concentration camp complex"), where three days later they were asked to step forward during roll call. No one did, and because the SS did not have their records, several of them survived.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b188–189-126)

### Tattoos and triangles

Further information: [Nazi concentration camp badge](/wiki/Nazi_concentration_camp_badge "Nazi concentration camp badge")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Auschwitz_outerwear_distinguish_yellow_Star_of_David.jpg/250px-Auschwitz_outerwear_distinguish_yellow_Star_of_David.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auschwitz_outerwear_distinguish_yellow_Star_of_David.jpg)

Auschwitz clothing

Uniquely at Auschwitz, prisoners were tattooed with a serial number, on their left breast for Soviet prisoners of war[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinbacher200590–91-127) and on the left arm for civilians.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGutman199820-128)[[123]](#cite_note-serialUSHHM-129) Categories of prisoner were distinguishable by triangular pieces of cloth (German: *Winkel*) sewn onto on their jackets below their prisoner number. Political prisoners *(Schutzhäftlinge* or Sch), mostly Poles, had a red triangle, while criminals (*Berufsverbrecher* or BV) were mostly German and wore green. Asocial prisoners (*Asoziale* or Aso), which included vagrants, prostitutes and the Roma, wore black. Purple was for Jehovah's Witnesses (*Internationale Bibelforscher-Vereinigung* or IBV)'s and pink for gay men, who were mostly German.[[124]](#cite_note-130) An estimated 5,000–15,000 gay men prosecuted under German Penal Code Section 175 (proscribing sexual acts between men) were detained in concentration camps, of whom an unknown number were sent to Auschwitz.[[125]](#cite_note-131) Jews wore a [yellow badge](/wiki/Yellow_badge "Yellow badge"), the shape of the [Star of David](/wiki/Star_of_David "Star of David"), overlaid by a second triangle if they also belonged to a second category. The nationality of the inmate was indicated by a letter stitched onto the cloth. A racial hierarchy existed, with German prisoners at the top. Next were non-Jewish prisoners from other countries. Jewish prisoners were at the bottom.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinbacher200531–32-132)

### Transports

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Freight_car%2C_Auschwitz_II-Birkenau%2C_2014.jpg/250px-Freight_car%2C_Auschwitz_II-Birkenau%2C_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Freight_car,_Auschwitz_II-Birkenau,_2014.jpg)

[Freight car](/wiki/Goods_wagon "Goods wagon") inside Auschwitz II-Birkenau, near the gatehouse, used to transport deportees, 2014[[127]](#cite_note-traincar-133)

Deportees were brought to Auschwitz crammed in wretched conditions into goods or cattle wagons, arriving near a railway station or at one of several dedicated trackside ramps, including one next to Auschwitz I. The *Altejudenrampe* (old Jewish ramp), part of the Oświęcim freight railway station, was used from 1942 to 1944 for Jewish transports.[[127]](#cite_note-traincar-133)[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIwaszko2000a17-134) Located between Auschwitz I and Auschwitz II, arriving at this ramp meant a 2.5 km journey to Auschwitz II and the gas chambers. Most deportees were forced to walk, accompanied by SS men and a car with a Red Cross symbol that carried the Zyklon B, as well as an SS doctor in case officers were poisoned by mistake. Inmates arriving at night, or who were too weak to walk, were taken by truck.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper1998c162-135) Work on a new railway line and ramp *(right)* between sectors BI and BII in Auschwitz II, was completed in May 1944 for the arrival of [Hungarian Jews](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Hungary#The_Holocaust "History of the Jews in Hungary")[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIwaszko2000a17-134) between May and early July 1944.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2010408-136) The rails led directly to the area around the gas chambers.[[127]](#cite_note-traincar-133)

### Life for the inmates

The day began at 4:30 am for the men (an hour later in winter), and earlier for the women, when the block supervisor sounded a gong and started beating inmates with sticks to make them wash and use the latrines quickly.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000b65–66-137) There were few latrines and there was a lack of clean water. Each washhouse had to service thousands of prisoners. In sectors BIa and BIb in Auschwitz II, two buildings containing latrines and washrooms were installed in 1943. These contained troughs for washing and 90 faucets; the toilet facilities were "sewage channels" covered by concrete with 58 holes for seating. There were three barracks with washing facilities or toilets to serve 16 residential barracks in BIIa, and six washrooms/latrines for 32 barracks in BIIb, BIIc, BIId, and BIIe.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIwaszko2000b56-138) [Primo Levi](/wiki/Primo_Levi "Primo Levi") described a 1944 [Auschwitz III](#Auschwitz_III–Monowitz) washroom:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Toaletter_p%C3%A5_auschwitz_2.jpg/250px-Toaletter_p%C3%A5_auschwitz_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toaletter_p%C3%A5_auschwitz_2.jpg)

[Latrine](/wiki/Latrine "Latrine") in the men's quarantine camp, sector BIIa, Auschwitz II, 2003

> It is badly lighted, full of draughts, with the brick floor covered by a layer of mud. The water is not drinkable; it has a revolting smell and often fails for many hours. The walls are covered by curious didactic [frescoes](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco"): for example, there is the good Häftling [prisoner], portrayed stripped to the waist, about to diligently soap his sheared and rosy cranium, and the bad Häftling, with a strong Semitic nose and a greenish colour, bundled up in his ostentatiously stained clothes with a beret on his head, who cautiously dips a finger into the water of the washbasin. Under the first is written: "*So bist du rein*" (like this you are clean), and under the second, "*So gehst du ein*" (like this you come to a bad end); and lower down, in doubtful French but in Gothic script: "*La propreté, c'est la santé*" [cleanliness is health].[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevi200145-139)

Prisoners received half a litre of coffee substitute or a herbal tea in the morning, but no food.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIwaszko2000b60-140) A second gong heralded roll call, when inmates lined up outside in rows of ten to be counted. No matter the weather, they had to wait for the SS to arrive for the count; how long they stood there depended on the officers' mood, and whether there had been escapes or other events attracting punishment.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000b66-141) Guards might force the prisoners to squat for an hour with their hands above their heads or hand out beatings or detention for infractions such as having a missing button or an improperly cleaned food bowl. The inmates were counted and re-counted.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinbacher200533-142)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Auschwitz_1_concentration_camp_bunks_6006_4162.jpg/250px-Auschwitz_1_concentration_camp_bunks_6006_4162.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auschwitz_1_concentration_camp_bunks_6006_4162.jpg)

Auschwitz II brick barracks, sector BI, 2006; four prisoners slept in each partition, known as a *buk*.[[137]](#cite_note-143)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Auschwitz.Birkenau.QuarantaineBarrack.jpg/250px-Auschwitz.Birkenau.QuarantaineBarrack.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auschwitz.Birkenau.QuarantaineBarrack.jpg)

Auschwitz II wooden barracks, 2008

After roll call, to the sound of "*Arbeitskommandos formieren*" ("form work details"), prisoners walked to their place of work, five abreast, to begin a working day that was normally 11 hours long—longer in summer and shorter in winter.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000b67-144) A prison orchestra, such as the [Women's Orchestra of Auschwitz](/wiki/Women%27s_Orchestra_of_Auschwitz "Women's Orchestra of Auschwitz"), was forced to play cheerful music as the workers left the camp. *Kapos* were responsible for the prisoners' behaviour while they worked, as was an SS escort. Much of the work took place outdoors at construction sites, gravel pits, and lumber yards. No rest periods were allowed. One prisoner was assigned to the latrines to measure the time the workers took to empty their bladders and bowels.[[139]](#cite_note-145)

Lunch was three-quarters of a litre of watery soup at midday, reportedly foul-tasting, with meat in the soup four times a week and vegetables (mostly potatoes and [rutabaga](/wiki/Rutabaga "Rutabaga")) three times. The evening meal was 300 grams of bread, often moldy, part of which the inmates were expected to keep for breakfast the next day, with a tablespoon of cheese or marmalade, or 25 grams of margarine or sausage. Prisoners engaged in hard labour were given extra rations.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIwaszko2000b60–61-146)

A second roll call took place at seven in the evening, in the course of which prisoners might be hanged or flogged. If a prisoner was missing, the others had to remain standing until the absentee was found or the reason for the absence discovered, even if it took hours. On 6 July 1940, roll call lasted 19 hours because a Polish prisoner, [Tadeusz Wiejowski](/wiki/Tadeusz_Wiejowski "Tadeusz Wiejowski"), had escaped; following an escape in 1941, a group of prisoners was picked out from the escapee's barracks and sent to block 11 to be starved to death.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000b68–69-147) After roll call, prisoners retired to their blocks for the night and received their bread rations. Then they had some free time to use the washrooms and receive their mail, unless they were Jews: Jews were not allowed to receive mail. Curfew ("nighttime quiet") was marked by a gong at nine o'clock.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000b69-148) Inmates slept in long rows of brick or wooden bunks, or on the floor, lying in and on their clothes and shoes to prevent them from being stolen.[[143]](#cite_note-149) The wooden bunks had blankets and paper mattresses filled with wood shavings; in the brick barracks, inmates lay on straw.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIwaszko2000b55-150) According to [Miklós Nyiszli](/wiki/Mikl%C3%B3s_Nyiszli "Miklós Nyiszli"):

> Eight hundred to a thousand people were crammed into the superimposed compartments of each barracks. Unable to stretch out completely, they slept there both lengthwise and crosswise, with one man's feet on another's head, neck, or chest. Stripped of all human dignity, they pushed and shoved and bit and kicked each other in an effort to get a few more inches' space on which to sleep a little more comfortably. For they did not have long to sleep.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENyiszli201125-151)

Sunday was not a workday, but prisoners had to clean the barracks and take their weekly shower,[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGutman199821-152) and were allowed to write (in German) to their families, although the SS censored the mail. Inmates who did not speak German would trade bread for help.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinbacher200534-153) [Observant Jews](/wiki/Shomer_Shabbat "Shomer Shabbat") tried to keep track of the [Hebrew calendar](/wiki/Hebrew_calendar "Hebrew calendar") and [Jewish holidays](/wiki/Jewish_holidays "Jewish holidays"), including [Shabbat](/wiki/Shabbat "Shabbat"), and the [weekly Torah portion](/wiki/Weekly_Torah_portion "Weekly Torah portion"). No watches, calendars, or clocks were permitted in the camp. Only two Jewish calendars made in Auschwitz survived to the end of the war. Prisoners kept track of the days in other ways, such as obtaining information from newcomers.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosen201418-154)

### Women's camp

See also: [Women's Orchestra of Auschwitz](/wiki/Women%27s_Orchestra_of_Auschwitz "Women's Orchestra of Auschwitz")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Female_prisoners_at_Birkenau.jpg/250px-Female_prisoners_at_Birkenau.jpg)](/wiki/File:Female_prisoners_at_Birkenau.jpg)

Women in Auschwitz II, May 1944

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Roll_call_at_Birkenau.jpg/250px-Roll_call_at_Birkenau.jpg)](/wiki/File:Roll_call_at_Birkenau.jpg)

Roll call in front of the kitchen building, Auschwitz II

About 30 percent of the registered inmates were female.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000c171-155) The first mass transport of women, 999 non-Jewish German women from the [Ravensbrück concentration camp](/wiki/Ravensbr%C3%BCck_concentration_camp "Ravensbrück concentration camp"), arrived on 26 March 1942. Classified as criminal, asocial and political, they were brought to Auschwitz as founder functionaries of the women's camp.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000143–144-156) Rudolf Höss wrote of them: "It was easy to predict that these beasts would mistreat the women over whom they exercised power ... Spiritual suffering was completely alien to them."[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000c177-157) They were given serial numbers 1–999.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000144-55)[[g]](#cite_note-158) The women's guard from Ravensbrück, [Johanna Langefeld](/wiki/Johanna_Langefeld "Johanna Langefeld"), became the first Auschwitz women's camp *Lagerführerin*.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000143–144-156) A second mass transport of women, 999 Jews from [Poprad](/wiki/Poprad "Poprad"), Slovakia, arrived on the same day. According to [Danuta Czech](/wiki/Danuta_Czech "Danuta Czech"), this was the first registered transport sent to Auschwitz by the [Reich Security Head Office](/wiki/Reich_Security_Head_Office "Reich Security Head Office") (RSHA) office IV B4, known as the Jewish Office, led by SS *Obersturmbannführer* [Adolf Eichmann](/wiki/Adolf_Eichmann "Adolf Eichmann").[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000144-55) (Office IV was the [Gestapo](/wiki/Gestapo "Gestapo").)[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStangneth201422-159) A third transport of 798 Jewish women from [Bratislava](/wiki/Bratislava "Bratislava"), Slovakia, followed on 28 March.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000144-55)

Women were at first held in blocks 1–10 of Auschwitz I,[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000c172-160) but from 6 August 1942,[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000155-161) 13,000 inmates were transferred to a new women's camp (*Frauenkonzentrationslager* or FKL) in Auschwitz II. This consisted at first of 15 brick and 15 wooden barracks in sector (*Bauabschnitt*) BIa; it was later extended into BIb,[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000c172–173-162) and by October 1943 it held 32,066 women.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzeleckaSetkiewicz200088-163) In 1943–1944, about 11,000 women were also housed in the [Gypsy family camp](#Gypsy_family_camp), as were several thousand in the [Theresienstadt family camp](#Theresienstadt_family_camp).[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000c174-164)

Conditions in the women's camp were so poor that when a group of male prisoners arrived to set up an infirmary in October 1942, their first task, according to researchers from the Auschwitz Museum, was to distinguish the corpses from the women who were still alive.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzeleckaSetkiewicz200088-163) [Gisella Perl](/wiki/Gisella_Perl "Gisella Perl"), a Romanian-Jewish gynecologist and inmate of the women's camp, wrote in 1948:

> There was one latrine for thirty to thirty-two thousand women and we were permitted to use it only at certain hours of the day. We stood in line to get in to this tiny building, knee-deep in human excrement. As we all suffered from dysentry, we could barely wait until our turn came, and soiled our ragged clothes, which never came off our bodies, thus adding to the horror of our existence by the terrible smell that surrounded us like a cloud. The latrine consisted of a deep ditch with planks thrown across it at certain intervals. We squatted on those planks like birds perched on a telegraph wire, so close together that we could not help soiling one another.[[158]](#cite_note-165)

Langefeld was succeeded as *Lagerführerin* in October 1942 by SS *Oberaufseherin* [Maria Mandl](/wiki/Maria_Mandl "Maria Mandl"), who developed a reputation for cruelty. Höss hired men to oversee the female supervisors, first SS *Obersturmführer* Paul Müller, then SS *Hauptsturmführer* [Franz Hössler](/wiki/Franz_H%C3%B6ssler "Franz Hössler").[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000c176-166) Mandl and Hössler were executed after the war. Sterilisation experiments were carried out in barracks 30 by a German gynecologist, [Carl Clauberg](/wiki/Carl_Clauberg "Carl Clauberg"), and another German doctor, [Horst Schumann](/wiki/Horst_Schumann "Horst Schumann").[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzeleckaSetkiewicz200088-163)

### Medical experiments, block 10

Main articles: [Block 10](/wiki/Block_10 "Block 10") and [Nazi human experimentation](/wiki/Nazi_human_experimentation "Nazi human experimentation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Auschwitz_Mengele_Block_10.jpg/250px-Auschwitz_Mengele_Block_10.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auschwitz_Mengele_Block_10.jpg)

[Block 10](/wiki/Block_10 "Block 10"), Auschwitz I, where medical experiments were performed on women

German doctors performed a variety of experiments on prisoners at Auschwitz. SS doctors tested the efficacy of [X-rays](/wiki/X-rays "X-rays") as a [sterilization](/wiki/Forced_sterilization "Forced sterilization") device by administering large doses to female prisoners. [Carl Clauberg](/wiki/Carl_Clauberg "Carl Clauberg") injected chemicals into women's [uteruses](/wiki/Uterus "Uterus") in an effort to glue them shut. Prisoners were infected with spotted fever for vaccination research and exposed to toxic substances to study the effects.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinbacher2005114–115-167) In one experiment, [Bayer](/wiki/Bayer "Bayer")—then part of [IG Farben](/wiki/IG_Farben "IG Farben")—paid RM 150 each for 150 female inmates from Auschwitz (the camp had asked for RM 200 per woman), who were transferred to a Bayer facility to test an anesthetic. A Bayer employee wrote to Rudolf Höss: "The transport of 150 women arrived in good condition. However, we were unable to obtain conclusive results because they died during the experiments. We would kindly request that you send us another group of women to the same number and at the same price." The Bayer research was led at Auschwitz by [Helmuth Vetter](/wiki/Helmuth_Vetter "Helmuth Vetter") of Bayer/IG Farben, who was also an Auschwitz physician and SS captain, and by Auschwitz physicians [Friedrich Entress](/wiki/Friedrich_Entress "Friedrich Entress") and [Eduard Wirths](/wiki/Eduard_Wirths "Eduard Wirths").[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000d362-168)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Doctors%27_trial%2C_Nuremberg%2C_1946%E2%80%931947.jpg/250px-Doctors%27_trial%2C_Nuremberg%2C_1946%E2%80%931947.jpg)](/wiki/File:Doctors%27_trial,_Nuremberg,_1946%E2%80%931947.jpg)

Defendants during the [Doctors' trial](/wiki/Doctors%27_trial "Doctors' trial"), Nuremberg, 1946–1947

The most infamous doctor at Auschwitz was [Josef Mengele](/wiki/Josef_Mengele "Josef Mengele"), the "Angel of Death", who worked in Auschwitz II from 30 May 1943, at first in the [gypsy family camp](#Gypsy_family_camp).[[162]](#cite_note-Kubica_1998_319-169) Interested in performing research on [identical twins](/wiki/Identical_twins "Identical twins"), [dwarfs](/wiki/Dwarfism "Dwarfism"), and those with hereditary disease, Mengele set up a kindergarten in barracks 29 and 31 for children he was experimenting on, and for all Romani children under six, where they were given better food rations.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKubica1998320–323-170) From May 1944, he would select twins and dwarfs from among the new arrivals during "selection",[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKubica1998325-171) reportedly calling for twins with "*Zwillinge heraus!*" ("twins step forward!").[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriedländer2007505-172) He and other doctors (the latter prisoners) would measure the twins' body parts, photograph them, and subject them to dental, sight and hearing tests, x-rays, blood tests, surgery, and blood transfusions between them.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKubica1998323–324-173) Then he would have them killed and dissected.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKubica1998325-171) [Kurt Heissmeyer](/wiki/Kurt_Heissmeyer "Kurt Heissmeyer"), another German doctor and SS officer, took 20 Polish Jewish children from Auschwitz to use in [pseudoscientific](/wiki/Pseudoscience "Pseudoscience") experiments at the [Neuengamme concentration camp](/wiki/Neuengamme_concentration_camp "Neuengamme concentration camp") near Hamburg, where he injected them with the [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis") [bacilli](/wiki/Bacilli "Bacilli") to test a cure for tuberculosis. In April 1945, the children were murdered by hanging to conceal the project.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKater2000124–125-174)

A [Jewish skeleton collection](/wiki/Jewish_skeleton_collection "Jewish skeleton collection") was obtained from among a pool of 115 Jewish inmates, chosen for their perceived stereotypical racial characteristics. [Rudolf Brandt](/wiki/Rudolf_Brandt "Rudolf Brandt") and [Wolfram Sievers](/wiki/Wolfram_Sievers "Wolfram Sievers"), general manager of the *[Ahnenerbe](/wiki/Ahnenerbe "Ahnenerbe")* (a Nazi research institute), delivered the skeletons to the collection of the Anatomy Institute at the [Reichsuniversität Straßburg](/wiki/Reichsuniversit%C3%A4t_Stra%C3%9Fburg "Reichsuniversität Straßburg") in [Alsace-Lorraine](/wiki/Alsace-Lorraine "Alsace-Lorraine"). The collection was sanctioned by [Heinrich Himmler](/wiki/Heinrich_Himmler "Heinrich Himmler") and under the direction of [August Hirt](/wiki/August_Hirt "August Hirt"). Ultimately 87 of the inmates were shipped to [Natzweiler-Struthof](/wiki/Natzweiler-Struthof "Natzweiler-Struthof") and murdered in August 1943.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005232–234-175) Brandt and Sievers were executed in 1948 after being convicted during the [Doctors' trial](/wiki/Doctors%27_trial "Doctors' trial"), part of the [Subsequent Nuremberg trials](/wiki/Subsequent_Nuremberg_trials "Subsequent Nuremberg trials").[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMehring2015161–163-176)

### Punishment, block 11

Main article: [Block 11](/wiki/Block_11 "Block 11")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Auschwitz_-_Blok_Smierci_and_The_Execution_Wall_Sk06_C_P.jpg/250px-Auschwitz_-_Blok_Smierci_and_The_Execution_Wall_Sk06_C_P.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auschwitz_-_Blok_Smierci_and_The_Execution_Wall_Sk06_C_P.jpg)

[Block 11](/wiki/Block_11 "Block 11") and the "death wall" *(left)*, Auschwitz I, 2000

Prisoners could be beaten and killed by guards and *kapos* for the slightest infraction of the rules. Polish historian Irena Strzelecka writes that *kapos* were given nicknames that reflected their sadism: "Bloody", "Iron", "The Strangler", "The Boxer".[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000d371–372-177) Based on the 275 extant reports of punishment in the Auschwitz archives, Strzelecka lists common infractions: returning a second time for food at mealtimes, removing one's gold teeth to buy bread, breaking into the pigsty to steal the pigs' food, putting one's hands into one's pockets.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000e373–376-178)

Flogging during rollcall was common. A flogging table called "the goat" immobilised prisoners' feet in a box, while they stretched themselves across the table. Prisoners had to count out the lashes—"25 mit besten Dank habe ich erhalten" ("25 received with many thanks")— and if they got the figure wrong, the flogging resumed from the beginning.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000e373–376-178) Punishment by "the post" involved tying prisoners' hands behind their backs with chains attached to hooks, then raising the chains so the prisoners were left dangling by the wrists. If their shoulders were too damaged afterwards to work, they might be sent to the gas chamber. Prisoners were subjected to the post for helping a prisoner who had been beaten, and for picking up a cigarette butt.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000e384–385-179) To extract information from inmates, guards would force their heads onto the stove, and hold them there, burning their faces and eyes.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000e389-180)

Known as block 13 until 1941, block 11 of Auschwitz I was the prison within the prison, reserved for inmates suspected of resistance activities.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000e381-181) Cell 22 in block 11 was a windowless [standing cell](/wiki/Standing_cell "Standing cell") (*Stehbunker*). Split into four sections, each section measured less than 1.0 m2 (11 sq ft) and held four prisoners, who entered it through a hatch near the floor. There was a 5 cm × 5 cm (2 in × 2 in) vent for air, covered by a perforated sheet. Strzelecka writes that prisoners might have to spend several nights in cell 22; Wiesław Kielar spent four weeks in it for breaking a pipe.[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000e382,_384-182) Several rooms in block 11 were deemed the *Polizei-Ersatz-Gefängnis Myslowitz in Auschwitz* (Auschwitz branch of the police station at [Mysłowice](/wiki/Mys%C5%82owice "Mysłowice")).[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b77-183) There were also *Sonderbehandlung* cases ("special treatment") for Poles and others regarded as dangerous to Nazi Germany.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b79-184)

### Death wall

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Poland-01334_-_Death_Wall_%2831387372450%29.jpg/250px-Poland-01334_-_Death_Wall_%2831387372450%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Poland-01334_-_Death_Wall_(31387372450).jpg)

The "death wall" showing the death-camp flag, the blue-and-white stripes with a red triangle signifying the Auschwitz uniform of political prisoners

The courtyard between blocks 10 and 11, known as the "death wall", served as an execution area, including for Poles in the General Government area who had been sentenced to death by a criminal court.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b79-184) The first executions, by shooting inmates in the back of the head, took place at the death wall on 11 November 1941, Poland's [National Independence Day](/wiki/National_Independence_Day_(Poland) "National Independence Day (Poland)"). The 151 accused were led to the wall one at a time, stripped naked and with their hands tied behind their backs. [Danuta Czech](/wiki/Danuta_Czech "Danuta Czech") noted that a "clandestine [Catholic mass](/wiki/Mass_in_the_Catholic_Church "Mass in the Catholic Church")" was said the following Sunday on the second floor of Block 4 in Auschwitz I, in a narrow space between bunks.[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000139-185)

An estimated 4,500 Polish political prisoners were executed at the death wall, including members of the camp resistance. An additional 10,000 Poles were brought to the camp to be executed without being registered. About 1,000 Soviet prisoners of war died by execution, although this is a rough estimate. A Polish government-in-exile report stated that 11,274 prisoners and 6,314 prisoners of war had been executed.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b102-186) Rudolf Höss wrote that "execution orders arrived in an unbroken stream".[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b77-183) According to SS officer [Perry Broad](/wiki/Perry_Broad "Perry Broad"), "[s]ome of these walking skeletons had spent months in the stinking cells, where not even animals would be kept, and they could barely manage to stand straight. And yet, at that last moment, many of them shouted 'Long live Poland', or 'Long live freedom'."[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b87-187) The dead included Colonel [Jan Karcz](/wiki/Jan_Karcz "Jan Karcz") and Major [Edward Gött-Getyński](/wiki/Edward_G%C3%B6tt-Gety%C5%84ski "Edward Gött-Getyński"), executed on 25 January 1943 with 51 others suspected of resistance activities. [Józef Noji](/wiki/J%C3%B3zef_Noji "Józef Noji"), the Polish long-distance runner, was executed on 15 February that year.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b89-188) In October 1944, 200 *Sonderkommando* were executed for their part in the [*Sonderkommando* revolt](#Sonderkommando_revolt).[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b89–90-189)

### Family camps

#### Gypsy family camp

Main articles: [Gypsy family camp (Auschwitz)](/wiki/Gypsy_family_camp_(Auschwitz) "Gypsy family camp (Auschwitz)") and [Romani genocide](/wiki/Romani_genocide "Romani genocide")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/d/d8/Romani_Kids_ww2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Romani_Kids_ww2.jpg)

[Romani](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") children, [Mulfingen](/wiki/Mulfingen "Mulfingen"), Germany, 1943; the children were studied by [Eva Justin](/wiki/Eva_Justin "Eva Justin") and later sent to Auschwitz.[[183]](#cite_note-190)

A separate camp for the [Roma](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people"), the *Zigeunerfamilienlager* ("Gypsy family camp"), was set up in the BIIe sector of Auschwitz II-Birkenau in February 1943. For unknown reasons, they were not subject to selection and families were allowed to stay together. The first transport of [German Roma](/wiki/Romani_people_in_Germany "Romani people in Germany") arrived on 26 February that year. There had been a small number of Romani inmates before that; two Czech Romani prisoners, Ignatz and Frank Denhel, tried to escape in December 1942, the latter successfully, and a Polish Romani woman, Stefania Ciuron, arrived on 12 February 1943 and escaped in April.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1998447–448-191) [Josef Mengele](/wiki/Josef_Mengele "Josef Mengele"), [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust")'s most infamous physician, worked in the gypsy family camp from 30 May 1943 when he began his work in Auschwitz.[[162]](#cite_note-Kubica_1998_319-169)
