The Auschwitz registry (*Hauptbücher*) shows that 20,946 Roma were registered prisoners,[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1998448-192) and another 3,000 are thought to have entered unregistered.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b55,_note_145-193) On 22 March 1943, one transport of 1,700 [Polish Sinti and Roma](/wiki/Romani_people_in_Poland "Romani people in Poland") was gassed on arrival because of illness, as was a second group of 1,035 on 25 May 1943.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1998448-192) The SS tried to liquidate the camp on 16 May 1944, but the Roma fought them, armed with knives and iron pipes, and the SS retreated. Shortly after this, the SS removed nearly 2,908 from the family camp to work, and on 2 August 1944 gassed the other 2,897. Ten thousand remain unaccounted for.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1998449–450-194)

#### Theresienstadt family camp

Main article: [Theresienstadt family camp](/wiki/Theresienstadt_family_camp "Theresienstadt family camp")

The SS deported around 18,000 Jews to Auschwitz from the [Theresienstadt Ghetto](/wiki/Theresienstadt_Ghetto "Theresienstadt Ghetto") in [Terezín](/wiki/Terez%C3%ADn "Terezín"), [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia"),[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzeleckaSetkiewicz200096-195) beginning on 8 September 1943 with a transport of 2,293 male and 2,713 female prisoners.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000185-196) Placed in sector BIIb as a "family camp", they were allowed to keep their belongings, wear their own clothes, and write letters to family; they did not have their hair shaved and were not subjected to selection.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzeleckaSetkiewicz200096-195) Correspondence between [Adolf Eichmann](/wiki/Adolf_Eichmann "Adolf Eichmann")'s office and the [International Red Cross](/wiki/International_Committee_of_the_Red_Cross "International Committee of the Red Cross") suggests that the Germans set up the camp to cast doubt on reports, in time for a planned Red Cross visit to Auschwitz, that mass murder was taking place there.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeren1998429-197) The women and girls were placed in odd-numbered barracks and the men and boys in even-numbered. An infirmary was set up in barracks 30 and 32, and barracks 31 became a school and kindergarten.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzeleckaSetkiewicz200096-195) The somewhat better living conditions were nevertheless inadequate; 1,000 members of the family camp were dead within six months.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeren1998428-198) Two other groups of 2,491 and 2,473 Jews arrived from Theresienstadt in the family camp on 16 and 20 December 1943.[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000190–191-199)

On 8 March 1944, 3,791 of the prisoners (men, women and children) were sent to the gas chambers; the men were taken to crematorium III and the women later to crematorium II.[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000194-200) Some of the groups were reported to have sung [Hatikvah](/wiki/Hatikvah "Hatikvah") and the Czech national anthem on the way.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeren1998439-201) Before they were murdered, they had been asked to write postcards to relatives, postdated to 25–27 March. Several twins were held back for medical experiments.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzeleckaSetkiewicz200097-202) The [Czechoslovak government-in-exile](/wiki/Czechoslovak_government-in-exile "Czechoslovak government-in-exile") initiated diplomatic manoeuvers to save the remaining Czech Jews after its representative in Bern received the [Vrba-Wetzler report](/wiki/Vrba-Wetzler_report "Vrba-Wetzler report"), written by two escaped prisoners, [Rudolf Vrba](/wiki/Rudolf_Vrba "Rudolf Vrba") and [Alfred Wetzler](/wiki/Alfred_Wetzler "Alfred Wetzler"), which warned that the remaining family-camp inmates would be gassed soon.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFleming2014231–232-203) The BBC also became aware of the report; its German service broadcast news of the family-camp murders during its women's programme on 16 June 1944, warning: "All those responsible for such massacres from top downwards will be called to account."[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFleming2014215-204) The Red Cross [visited Theresienstadt](/wiki/Maurice_Rossel#Rossel's_report "Maurice Rossel") in June 1944 and were persuaded by the SS that no one was being deported from there.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeren1998429-197) The following month, about 2,000 women from the family camp were selected to be moved to other camps and 80 boys were moved to the men's camp; the remaining 7,000 were gassed between 10 and 12 July.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000203-205)

## Selection and extermination process

### Gas chambers

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Crematorium_at_Auschwitz_I_2012.jpg/330px-Crematorium_at_Auschwitz_I_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crematorium_at_Auschwitz_I_2012.jpg)

A reconstruction of crematorium I, Auschwitz I, 2014[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDworkvan_Pelt2002363-206)

The first gassings at Auschwitz took place on 3 September 1941, when around 850 inmates—Soviet prisoners of war and sick Polish inmates—were killed with Zyklon B in the basement of [block 11](/wiki/Block_11 "Block 11") in Auschwitz I. The building proved unsuitable, so gassings were conducted instead in crematorium I, also in Auschwitz I, which operated until December 1942. There, more than 700 victims could be killed at once.[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper1998c157–159-207) Tens of thousands were killed in crematorium I.[[53]](#cite_note-Müller_1999_31-59) To keep the victims calm, they were told they were to undergo disinfection and [de-lousing](/wiki/Delousing "Delousing"); they were ordered to undress outside, then were locked in the building and gassed. After its decommissioning as a gas chamber, the building was converted to a storage facility and later served as an SS air raid shelter.[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper1998c159–160-208) The gas chamber and crematorium were reconstructed after the war. Dwork and van Pelt write that a chimney was recreated; four openings in the roof were installed to show where the Zyklon B had entered; and two of the three furnaces were rebuilt with the original components.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDworkvan_Pelt2002364-36)[[202]](#cite_note-209)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Selection_on_the_ramp_at_Auschwitz_II-Birkenau%2C_1944_%28Auschwitz_Album%29_3b.jpg/250px-Selection_on_the_ramp_at_Auschwitz_II-Birkenau%2C_1944_%28Auschwitz_Album%29_3b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Selection_on_the_ramp_at_Auschwitz_II-Birkenau,_1944_(Auschwitz_Album)_3b.jpg)

Hungarian Jews arriving at Auschwitz II, May/June 1944

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Arrival_platform_at_Birkenau.jpg/250px-Arrival_platform_at_Birkenau.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arrival_platform_at_Birkenau.jpg)

Crematoria II and III and their chimneys are visible in the background, left and right.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Birkenau_a_group_of_Jews_walking_towards_the_gas_chambers_and_crematoria.jpg/250px-Birkenau_a_group_of_Jews_walking_towards_the_gas_chambers_and_crematoria.jpg)](/wiki/File:Birkenau_a_group_of_Jews_walking_towards_the_gas_chambers_and_crematoria.jpg)

Jewish women and children from Hungary walking toward the gas chamber, Auschwitz II, May/June 1944. The gate on the left leads to sector BI, the oldest part of the camp.[[203]](#cite_note-210)

In early 1942, mass exterminations were moved to two provisional gas chambers (the "red house" and "white house", known as bunkers 1 and 2) in Auschwitz II, while the larger crematoria (II, III, IV, and V) were under construction. Bunker 2 was temporarily reactivated from May to November 1944, when large numbers of Hungarian Jews were gassed.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper1998c161–162-211) In summer 1944 the combined capacity of the crematoria and outdoor incineration pits was 20,000 bodies per day.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper1998c174-212) A planned sixth facility—crematorium VI—was never built.[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper1998c175-213)

From 1942, Jews were being transported to Auschwitz from all over German-occupied Europe by rail, arriving in daily convoys.[[207]](#cite_note-214) The gas chambers worked to their fullest capacity from May to July 1944, during the [Holocaust in Hungary](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Hungary#History_of_the_Jews_in_Hungary "History of the Jews in Hungary").[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2010407-215) A rail spur leading to crematoria II and III in Auschwitz II was completed that May, and a new ramp was built between sectors BI and BII to deliver the victims closer to the gas chambers (images top right). On 29 April the first 1,800 Jews from Hungary arrived at the camp.[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDworkvan_Pelt2002338-216) From 14 May until early July 1944, 437,000 Hungarian Jews, half the pre-war population, were deported to Auschwitz, at a rate of 12,000 a day for a considerable part of that period.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2010408-136) The crematoria had to be overhauled. Crematoria II and III were given new elevators leading from the stoves to the gas chambers, new grates were fitted, and several of the dressing rooms and gas chambers were painted. Cremation pits were dug behind crematorium V.[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDworkvan_Pelt2002338-216) The incoming volume was so great that the *Sonderkommando* resorted to burning corpses in open-air pits as well as in the crematoria.[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDworkvan_Pelt2002341–343-217)

### Selection

According to Polish historian [Franciszek Piper](/wiki/Franciszek_Piper "Franciszek Piper"), of the 1,095,000 Jews deported to Auschwitz, around 205,000 were registered in the camp and given serial numbers; 25,000 were sent to other camps; and 865,000 were murdered soon after arrival.[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b227-218) Adding non-Jewish victims gives a figure of 900,000 who were murdered without being registered.[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b229-219)

During "selection" on arrival, those deemed able to work were sent to the right and admitted into the camp (registered), and the rest were sent to the left to be gassed. The group selected to die included almost all children, women with small children, the elderly, and others who appeared on brief and superficial inspection by an SS doctor not to be fit for work.[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b103ff-220) Practically any fault—scars, bandages, boils and emaciation—might provide reason enough to be deemed unfit.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b109–110-221) Children might be made to walk toward a stick held at a certain height; those who could walk under it were selected for the gas.[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b111-222) Inmates unable to walk or who arrived at night were taken to the crematoria on trucks; otherwise, the new arrivals were marched there.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper1998c162,_169-223) Their belongings were seized and sorted by inmates in the ["Kanada" warehouses](/wiki/Kanada_warehouses,_Auschwitz "Kanada warehouses, Auschwitz"), an area of the camp in sector BIIg that housed 30 barracks used as storage facilities for plundered goods; it derived its name from the inmates' view of Canada as a land of plenty.[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzeleckaSetkiewicz200097–98-224)

### Inside the crematoria

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Entrance_to_Crematorium_III_in_Auschwitz_II_%28Birkenau%29.jpg/250px-Entrance_to_Crematorium_III_in_Auschwitz_II_%28Birkenau%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Entrance_to_Crematorium_III_in_Auschwitz_II_(Birkenau).jpg)

Entrance to crematorium III, Auschwitz II, 2008[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaxter2017241-225)

The crematoria consisted of a dressing room, gas chamber, and furnace room. In crematoria II and III, the dressing room and gas chamber were underground; in IV and V, they were on the ground floor. The dressing room had numbered hooks on the wall to hang clothes. In crematorium II, there was also a dissection room (*Sezierraum*).[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper1998c166,_168-226) SS officers told the victims they had to take a shower and undergo delousing. The victims undressed in the dressing room and walked into the gas chamber; signs said "Bade" (bath) or "Desinfektionsraum" (disinfection room). A former prisoner testified that the language of the signs changed depending on who was being killed.[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b169,_n. 489-227) A gas chamber could hold up to 2,000.[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b169-228)

The Zyklon B was delivered to the crematoria by a special SS bureau known as the Hygiene Institute.[[222]](#cite_note-229) After the doors were shut, SS men dumped in the Zyklon B pellets through vents in the roof or holes in the side of the chamber. The victims were usually dead within 10 minutes; Rudolf Höss testified that it took up to 20 minutes.[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b170-230) [Leib Langfus](/wiki/Leib_Langfus "Leib Langfus"), a member of the *Sonderkommando*, buried his diary (written in [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish "Yiddish")) near crematorium III in Auschwitz II. It was found in 1952, signed "A.Y.R.A":[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECohen1998529,_531-231)

> It would be difficult to even imagine that so many people would fit in such a small [room]. Anyone who did not want to go inside was shot [...] or torn apart by the dogs. They would have suffocated from the lack of air within several hours. Then all the doors were sealed tight and the gas thrown in by way of a small hole in the ceiling. There was nothing more that the people inside could do. And so they only screamed in bitter, lamentable voices. Others complained in voices full of despair, and others still sobbed spasmodically and sent up a dire, heart-rending weeping. ... And in the meantime, their voices grew weaker and weaker ... Because of the great crowding, people fell one atop another as they died, until a heap arose consisting of five or six layers atop the other, reaching a height of one meter. Mothers froze in a seated position on the ground embracing their children in their arms, and husbands and wives died hugging each other. Some of the people made up a formless mass. Others stood in a leaning position, while the upper parts, from the stomach up, were in a lying position. Some of the people had turned completely blue under the influence of the gas, while others looks entirely fresh, as if they were asleep.[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELangfus2000357-232)

### Use of corpses

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Auschwitz_Resistance_282_cropped.JPG/250px-Auschwitz_Resistance_282_cropped.JPG)](/wiki/File:Auschwitz_Resistance_282_cropped.JPG)

One of the [*Sonderkommando* photographs](/wiki/Sonderkommando_photographs "Sonderkommando photographs"): Women on their way to the gas chamber, Auschwitz II, August 1944

*Sonderkommando* wearing gas masks dragged the bodies from the chamber. They removed glasses and artificial limbs and shaved off the women's hair;[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b170-230) women's hair was removed before they entered the gas chamber at [Bełżec](/wiki/Belzec_extermination_camp "Belzec extermination camp"), [Sobibór](/wiki/Sobibor_extermination_camp "Sobibor extermination camp"), and [Treblinka](/wiki/Treblinka_extermination_camp "Treblinka extermination camp"), but at Auschwitz it was done after death.[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecki2000b408-233) By 6 February 1943, the Reich Economic Ministry had received 3,000 kg of women's hair from Auschwitz and [Majdanek](/wiki/Majdanek_concentration_camp "Majdanek concentration camp").[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecki2000b408-233) The hair was first cleaned in a solution of [sal ammoniac](/wiki/Sal_ammoniac "Sal ammoniac"), dried on the brick floor of the crematoria, combed, and placed in paper bags.[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecki2000b409-234) The hair was shipped to various companies, including one manufacturing plant in [Bremen-Bluementhal](/wiki/Bremen "Bremen"), where workers found tiny coins with Greek letters on some of the braids, possibly from some of the 50,000 Greek Jews deported to Auschwitz in 1943.[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecki2000b411-235) When they liberated the camp in January 1945, the Red Army found 7,000 kg of human hair in bags ready to ship.[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecki2000b409-234)

Just before cremation, jewelry was removed, along with dental work and teeth containing precious metals.[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b171-236) Gold was removed from the teeth of dead prisoners from 23 September 1940 onwards by order of Heinrich Himmler.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecki2000b400-237) The work was carried out by members of the *Sonderkommando* who were dentists; anyone overlooking dental work might themselves be cremated alive.[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b171-236) The gold was sent to the SS Health Service and used by dentists to treat the SS and their families; 50 kg had been collected by 8 October 1942.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecki2000b400-237) By early 1944, 10–12 kg of gold was being extracted monthly from victims' teeth.[[231]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecki2000b406-238)

The corpses were burned in the nearby incinerators, and the ashes were buried, thrown in the [Vistula](/wiki/Vistula "Vistula") river, or used as fertilizer. Any bits of bone that had not burned properly were ground down in wooden [mortars](/wiki/Mortar_and_pestle "Mortar and pestle").[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper1998c171-239)

### Death toll

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Arrivals_and_inmates_on_the_ramp_at_Auschwitz-Birkenau%2C_summer_1944_%28Auschwitz_Album%29.jpg/250px-Arrivals_and_inmates_on_the_ramp_at_Auschwitz-Birkenau%2C_summer_1944_%28Auschwitz_Album%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arrivals_and_inmates_on_the_ramp_at_Auschwitz-Birkenau,_summer_1944_(Auschwitz_Album).jpg)

New arrivals, Auschwitz II-Birkenau, May/June 1944

At least 1.3 million people were sent to Auschwitz between 1940 and 1945, and at least 1.1 million died.[[8]](#cite_note-Piperfigures-8) Overall 400,207 prisoners were registered in the camp: 268,657 male and 131,560 female.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecka2000c171-155) A study in the late 1980s by Polish historian [Franciszek Piper](/wiki/Franciszek_Piper "Franciszek Piper"), published by [Yad Vashem](/wiki/Yad_Vashem "Yad Vashem") in 1991,[[233]](#cite_note-240) used timetables of train arrivals combined with deportation records to calculate that, of the 1.3 million sent to the camp, 1,082,000 had died there, a figure (rounded up to 1.1 million) that Piper regarded as a minimum.[[8]](#cite_note-Piperfigures-8) That figure came to be widely accepted.[[h]](#cite_note-244)

The Germans tried to conceal how many they had murdered. In July 1942, according to Rudolf Höss's post-war memoir, Höss received an order from [Heinrich Himmler](/wiki/Heinrich_Himmler "Heinrich Himmler"), via [Adolf Eichmann](/wiki/Adolf_Eichmann "Adolf Eichmann")'s office and SS commander [Paul Blobel](/wiki/Paul_Blobel "Paul Blobel"), that "[a]ll mass graves were to be opened and the corpses burned. In addition, the ashes were to be disposed of in such a way that it would be impossible at some future time to calculate the number of corpses burned."[[237]](#cite_note-245)

Earlier estimates of the death toll were higher than Piper's. Following the camp's liberation, the Soviet government issued a statement, on 8 May 1945, that four million people had been murdered on the site, a figure based on the capacity of the crematoria.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b210–213-246) Höss told prosecutors at Nuremberg that at least 2,500,000 people had been gassed there, and that another 500,000 had died of starvation and disease.[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_International_Military_Tribunal,_Nuremberg1946415-247) He testified that the figure of over two million had come from Eichmann.[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_International_Military_Tribunal,_Nuremberg1946397-248) In his memoirs, written in custody, Höss wrote that Eichmann had given the figure of 2.5 million to Höss's superior officer [Richard Glücks](/wiki/Richard_Gl%C3%BCcks "Richard Glücks"), based on records that had been destroyed.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHöss2003193-249) Höss regarded this figure as "far too high. Even Auschwitz had limits to its destructive possibilities," he wrote.[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHöss2003194-250)

| Nationality/ethnicity (Source: [Franciszek Piper](/wiki/Franciszek_Piper "Franciszek Piper"))[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b230-2) | Registered deaths (Auschwitz) | Unregistered deaths (Auschwitz) | Total |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Jews](/wiki/Jew "Jew") | 95,000 | 865,000 | 960,000 |
| [Ethnic Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") | 64,000 | 10,000 | 74,000 (70,000–75,000) |
| [Roma](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") and [Sinti](/wiki/Sinti "Sinti") | 19,000 | 2,000 | 21,000 |
| [Soviet prisoners of war](/wiki/German_mistreatment_of_Soviet_prisoners_of_war "German mistreatment of Soviet prisoners of war") | 12,000 | 3,000 | 15,000 |
| Other [Europeans](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Europe "Ethnic groups in Europe"): [Soviet citizens](/wiki/Soviet_people "Soviet people") ([Byelorussians](/wiki/Byelorussian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Byelorussian Soviet Socialist Republic"), [Russians](/wiki/Russians "Russians"), [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians")), [Czechs](/wiki/Czechs "Czechs"), [Yugoslavs](/wiki/Yugoslavs "Yugoslavs"), [French](/wiki/French_people "French people"), [Germans](/wiki/Germans "Germans"), [Austrians](/wiki/Austrians "Austrians") | 10,000–15,000 | n/a | 10,000–15,000 |
| **Total deaths in Auschwitz, 1940–1945** | 200,000–205,000 | 880,000 | 1,080,000–1,085,000 |

Around one in six Jews murdered in the Holocaust died in Auschwitz.[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnyder2010383-251) By nation, the greatest number of Auschwitz's Jewish victims originated from Hungary, accounting for 430,000 deaths, followed by Poland (300,000), France (69,000), Netherlands (60,000), Greece (55,000), Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia (46,000), Slovakia (27,000), Belgium (25,000), Germany and Austria (23,000), Yugoslavia (10,000), Italy (7,500), Norway (690), and others (34,000).[[244]](#cite_note-Ethnicity-252) [Timothy Snyder](/wiki/Timothy_Snyder "Timothy Snyder") writes that fewer than one percent of the million Soviet Jews murdered in the Holocaust were murdered in Auschwitz.[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnyder2010275-253) Of the at least 387 Jehovah's Witnesses who were imprisoned at Auschwitz, 132 died in the camp.[[246]](#cite_note-254)

### Camp resistance, flow of information

* [!["The Mass Extermination of Jews in German Occupied Poland", a paper issued by the Polish government-in-exile addressed to the United Nations, 1942](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/The_Mass_Extermination_of_Jews_in_German_Occupied.pdf/page1-250px-The_Mass_Extermination_of_Jews_in_German_Occupied.pdf.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Mass_Extermination_of_Jews_in_German_Occupied.pdf "\"The Mass Extermination of Jews in German Occupied Poland\", a paper issued by the Polish government-in-exile addressed to the United Nations, 1942")

  "[The Mass Extermination of Jews in German Occupied Poland](/wiki/The_Mass_Extermination_of_Jews_in_German_Occupied_Poland "The Mass Extermination of Jews in German Occupied Poland")", a paper issued by the [Polish government-in-exile](/wiki/Polish_government-in-exile "Polish government-in-exile") addressed to the [United Nations](/wiki/Declaration_by_United_Nations "Declaration by United Nations"), 1942

Information about Auschwitz became available to the Allies as a result of reports by Captain [Witold Pilecki](/wiki/Witold_Pilecki "Witold Pilecki") of the Polish [Home Army](/wiki/Home_Army "Home Army")[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFleming2014131-257) who, as "Tomasz Serafiński" (serial number 4859),[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000177-258) allowed himself to be arrested in Warsaw and taken to Auschwitz.[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFleming2014131-257) He was imprisoned there from 22 September 1940[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartrop2016210-259) until his escape on 27 April 1943.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000177-258) [Michael Fleming](/wiki/Michael_Fleming_(historian) "Michael Fleming (historian)") writes that Pilecki was instructed to sustain morale, organize food, clothing and resistance, prepare to take over the camp if possible, and smuggle information out to the Polish military.[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFleming2014131-257) Pilecki called his resistance movement [Związek Organizacji Wojskowej](/wiki/Zwi%C4%85zek_Organizacji_Wojskowej "Związek Organizacji Wojskowej") (ZOW, "Union of Military Organization").[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartrop2016210-259)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Witold_Pilecki_in_color.jpg/250px-Witold_Pilecki_in_color.jpg)](/wiki/File:Witold_Pilecki_in_color.jpg)

Captain [Witold Pilecki](/wiki/Witold_Pilecki "Witold Pilecki")

The resistance sent out the first oral message about Auschwitz with Aleksander Wielkopolski, a Polish engineer who was released in October 1940.[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEŚwiebocki200068–69,_n._115-260) The following month the Polish underground in Warsaw prepared a report on the basis of that information, *The camp in Auschwitz*, part of which was published in London in May 1941 in a booklet, *The German Occupation of Poland*, by the Polish Ministry of Foreign Affairs. The report said of the Jews in the camp that "scarcely any of them came out alive". According to Fleming, the booklet was "widely circulated amongst British officials". The *Polish Fortnightly Review* based a story on it, writing that "three crematorium furnaces were insufficient to cope with the bodies being cremated", as did *[The Scotsman](/wiki/The_Scotsman "The Scotsman")* on 8 January 1942, the only British news organization to do so.[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFleming2014131–132-261)

On 24 December 1941, the resistance groups representing the various prisoner factions met in block 45 and agreed to cooperate. Fleming writes that it has not been possible to track Pilecki's early intelligence from the camp. Pilecki compiled two reports after he escaped in April 1943; the second, [Raport W](/wiki/Raport_W "Raport W"), detailed his life in Auschwitz I and estimated that 1.5 million people, mostly Jews, had been murdered.[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFleming2014132-262) On 1 July 1942, the *Polish Fortnightly Review* published a report describing Birkenau, writing that "prisoners call this supplementary camp 'Paradisal', presumably because there is only one road, leading to Paradise". Reporting that inmates were being killed "through excessive work, torture and medical means", it noted the gassing of the Soviet prisoners of war and Polish inmates in Auschwitz I in September 1941, the first gassing in the camp. It said: "It is estimated that the Oswiecim camp can accommodate fifteen thousand prisoners, but as they die on a mass scale there is always room for new arrivals."[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFleming2014133-263)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/P_Oboz.jpg/250px-P_Oboz.jpg)](/wiki/File:P_Oboz.jpg)

The camp badge for non-Jewish Polish political prisoners

The [Polish government-in-exile](/wiki/Polish_government-in-exile "Polish government-in-exile") in London first reported the gassing of prisoners in Auschwitz on 21 July 1942,[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinbacher2005116-264) and reported the gassing of Soviet POWs and Jews on 4 September 1942.[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFleming2014135-265) In 1943, the *[Kampfgruppe Auschwitz](/wiki/Kampfgruppe_Auschwitz "Kampfgruppe Auschwitz")* (Combat Group Auschwitz) was organized within the camp with the aim of sending out information about what was happening.[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaisEngelFogelman200773-266) The *Sonderkommando* buried notes in the ground, hoping they would be found by the camp's liberators.[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENyiszli2011124-267) The group also smuggled out photographs; the [*Sonderkommando* photographs](/wiki/Sonderkommando_photographs "Sonderkommando photographs"), of events around the gas chambers in Auschwitz II, were smuggled out of the camp in September 1944 in a toothpaste tube.[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDidi-Huberman200816-268)

According to Fleming, the British press responded, in 1943 and the first half of 1944, either by not publishing reports about Auschwitz or by burying them on the inside pages. The exception was the *Polish Jewish Observer*, a *[City and East London Observer](/wiki/City_and_East_London_Observer "City and East London Observer")* supplement edited by Joel Cang, a former Warsaw correspondent for the *[Manchester Guardian](/wiki/Manchester_Guardian "Manchester Guardian")*. The British reticence stemmed from a Foreign Office concern that the public might pressure the government to respond or provide refuge for the Jews, and that British actions on behalf of the Jews might affect its relationships in the Middle East. There was similar reticence in the United States, and indeed within the Polish government-in-exile and the Polish resistance. According to Fleming, the scholarship suggests that the Polish resistance distributed information about the Holocaust in Auschwitz without challenging the Allies' reluctance to highlight it.[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFleming201663–65-269)

### Escapes, *Auschwitz Protocols*

Further information: [Vrba-Wetzler report](/wiki/Vrba-Wetzler_report "Vrba-Wetzler report") and [Auschwitz Protocols](/wiki/Auschwitz_Protocols "Auschwitz Protocols")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Telegram%2C_Vrba_and_Wetzler_escape%2C_Auschwitz%2C_8_April_1944.jpg/250px-Telegram%2C_Vrba_and_Wetzler_escape%2C_Auschwitz%2C_8_April_1944.jpg)](/wiki/File:Telegram,_Vrba_and_Wetzler_escape,_Auschwitz,_8_April_1944.jpg)

Telegram dated 8 April 1944 from *KL Auschwitz* reporting the escape of [Rudolf Vrba](/wiki/Rudolf_Vrba "Rudolf Vrba") and [Alfréd Wetzler](/wiki/Alfr%C3%A9d_Wetzler "Alfréd Wetzler")

From the first escape on 6 July 1940 of [Tadeusz Wiejowski](/wiki/Tadeusz_Wiejowski "Tadeusz Wiejowski"), at least 802 prisoners (757 men and 45 women) tried to escape from the camp, according to Polish historian [Henryk Świebocki](/wiki/Henryk_%C5%9Awiebocki "Henryk Świebocki").[[262]](#cite_note-270)[[i]](#cite_note-272) He writes that most escapes were attempted from work sites outside the camp's perimeter fence.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEŚwiebocki2000192-273) Of the 802 escapes, 144 were successful, 327 were caught, and the fate of 331 is unknown.[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEŚwiebocki2000233-271)

Four Polish prisoners—[Eugeniusz Bendera](/w/index.php?title=Eugeniusz_Bendera&action=edit&redlink=1 "Eugeniusz Bendera (page does not exist)") [[pl](https://pl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eugeniusz_Bendera "pl:Eugeniusz Bendera")] (serial number 8502), [Kazimierz Piechowski](/wiki/Kazimierz_Piechowski "Kazimierz Piechowski") (no. 918), [Stanisław Gustaw Jaster](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_Gustaw_Jaster "Stanisław Gustaw Jaster") (no. 6438), and Józef Lempart (no. 3419)—escaped successfully on 20 June 1942. After breaking into a warehouse, three of them dressed as SS officers and stole rifles and an SS staff car, which they drove out of the camp with the fourth handcuffed as a prisoner. They wrote later to Rudolf Höss apologizing for the loss of the vehicle.[[265]](#cite_note-274) On 21 July 1944, Polish inmate [Jerzy Bielecki](/wiki/Jerzy_Bielecki_(Auschwitz_survivor) "Jerzy Bielecki (Auschwitz survivor)") dressed in an SS uniform and, using a faked pass, managed to cross the camp's gate with his Jewish girlfriend, Cyla Cybulska, pretending that she was wanted for questioning. Both survived the war. For having saved her, Bielecki was recognized by [Yad Vashem](/wiki/Yad_Vashem "Yad Vashem") as [Righteous Among the Nations](/wiki/Righteous_Among_the_Nations "Righteous Among the Nations").[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEŚwiebocki2000203–204-275)

[Jerzy Tabeau](/wiki/Jerzy_Tabeau "Jerzy Tabeau") (no. 27273, registered as Jerzy Wesołowski) and Roman Cieliczko (no. 27089), both Polish prisoners, escaped on 19 November 1943; Tabeau made contact with the Polish underground and, between December 1943 and early 1944, wrote what became known as the *Polish Major's report* about the situation in the camp.[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEŚwiebocki200212–13,_23-276) On 27 April 1944, [Rudolf Vrba](/wiki/Rudolf_Vrba "Rudolf Vrba") (no. 44070) and [Alfréd Wetzler](/wiki/Alfr%C3%A9d_Wetzler "Alfréd Wetzler") (no. 29162) escaped to Slovakia, carrying detailed information to the [Slovak Jewish Council](/wiki/Slovak_Jewish_Council "Slovak Jewish Council") about the gas chambers. The distribution of the [Vrba-Wetzler report](/wiki/Vrba-Wetzler_report "Vrba-Wetzler report"), and [publication of parts of it](/wiki/Rudolf_Vrba#News_coverage "Rudolf Vrba") in June 1944, helped to halt the [deportation of Hungarian Jews](/wiki/Holocaust_in_Hungary "Holocaust in Hungary") to Auschwitz. On 27 May 1944, Arnost Rosin (no. 29858) and [Czesław Mordowicz](/wiki/Czes%C5%82aw_Mordowicz "Czesław Mordowicz") (no. 84216) also escaped to Slovakia; the Rosin-Mordowicz report was added to the Vrba-Wetzler and Tabeau reports to become what is known as the *[Auschwitz Protocols](/wiki/Auschwitz_Protocols "Auschwitz Protocols")*.[[268]](#cite_note-277) The reports were first published in their entirety in November 1944 by the United States [War Refugee Board](/wiki/War_Refugee_Board "War Refugee Board") as *The Extermination Camps of Auschwitz (Oświęcim) and Birkenau in Upper Silesia*.[[269]](#cite_note-278)

### Bombing proposal

Main article: [Auschwitz bombing debate](/wiki/Auschwitz_bombing_debate "Auschwitz bombing debate")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Auschwitz_aerial_view_RAF.jpg/250px-Auschwitz_aerial_view_RAF.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auschwitz_aerial_view_RAF.jpg)

Aerial view of Auschwitz II-Birkenau taken by the [RAF](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force") on 23 August 1944

In January 1941, the Commander-in-Chief of the [Polish Army](/wiki/Polish_Armed_Forces "Polish Armed Forces") and prime minister-in-exile, [Władysław Sikorski](/wiki/W%C5%82adys%C5%82aw_Sikorski "Władysław Sikorski"), arranged for a report to be forwarded to Air Marshal [Richard Pierse](/wiki/Richard_Pierse "Richard Pierse"), head of [RAF](/wiki/RAF "RAF") [Bomber Command](/wiki/Bomber_Command "Bomber Command").[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiddle200036-279) Written by Auschwitz prisoners in or around December 1940, the report described the camp's atrocious living conditions and asked the [Polish government-in-exile](/wiki/Polish_government-in-exile "Polish government-in-exile") to bomb it:

> The prisoners implore the Polish Government to have the camp bombed. The destruction of the electrified barbed wire, the ensuing panic and darkness prevailing, the chances of escape would be great. The local population will hide them and help them to leave the neighbourhood. The prisoners are confidently awaiting the day when Polish planes from Great Britain will enable their escape. This is the prisoners unanimous demand to the Polish Government in London.[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWestermann2004197-280)

Pierse replied that it was not technically feasible to bomb the camp without harming the prisoners.[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiddle200036-279) In May 1944 Slovak rabbi [Michael Dov Weissmandl](/wiki/Michael_Dov_Weissmandl "Michael Dov Weissmandl") suggested that the Allies bomb the rails leading to the camp.[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitchens200080–81-281) Historian [David Wyman](/wiki/David_Wyman "David Wyman") published an essay in *[Commentary](/wiki/Commentary_(magazine) "Commentary (magazine)")* in 1978 entitled "Why Auschwitz Was Never Bombed", arguing that the [United States Army Air Forces](/wiki/United_States_Army_Air_Forces "United States Army Air Forces") could and should have attacked Auschwitz. In his book *[The Abandonment of the Jews: America and the Holocaust 1941–1945](/wiki/The_Abandonment_of_the_Jews "The Abandonment of the Jews")* (1984), Wyman argued that, since the IG Farben plant at Auschwitz III had been bombed three times between August and December 1944 by the US [Fifteenth Air Force](/wiki/Fifteenth_Air_Force "Fifteenth Air Force") in Italy, it would have been feasible for the other camps or railway lines to be bombed too. [Bernard Wasserstein](/wiki/Bernard_Wasserstein "Bernard Wasserstein")'s *Britain and the Jews of Europe* (1979) and [Martin Gilbert](/wiki/Martin_Gilbert "Martin Gilbert")'s *Auschwitz and the Allies* (1981) raised similar questions about British inaction.[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeufeld20001–2-282) Since the 1990s, other historians have argued that Allied bombing accuracy was not sufficient for Wyman's proposed attack, and that [counterfactual history](/wiki/Counterfactual_history "Counterfactual history") is an inherently problematic endeavor.[[274]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeufeld20004–5,_9–10-283)

### *Sonderkommando* revolt

Main article: [Sonderkommando Revolt in Auschwitz](/wiki/Sonderkommando_Revolt_in_Auschwitz "Sonderkommando Revolt in Auschwitz")

Further information: [Sonderkommando § Auschwitz](/wiki/Sonderkommando#Auschwitz "Sonderkommando")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/%D7%96%D7%9C%D7%9E%D7%9F_%D7%92%D7%A8%D7%93%D7%95%D7%91%D7%A1%D7%A7%D7%99.jpg/250px-%D7%96%D7%9C%D7%9E%D7%9F_%D7%92%D7%A8%D7%93%D7%95%D7%91%D7%A1%D7%A7%D7%99.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D7%96%D7%9C%D7%9E%D7%9F_%D7%92%D7%A8%D7%93%D7%95%D7%91%D7%A1%D7%A7%D7%99.jpg)

*[Sonderkommando](/wiki/Sonderkommando "Sonderkommando")* member [Zalmen Gradowski](/wiki/Zalman_Gradowski "Zalman Gradowski"), pictured with his wife, Sonia, buried his notebooks near crematorium III. Sonia Gradowski was gassed on 8 December 1942.[[275]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGradowski1989548-284)

The *[Sonderkommando](/wiki/Sonderkommando "Sonderkommando")* who worked in the crematoria were witnesses to the mass murder and were therefore regularly murdered themselves.[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b181–187-285) On 7 October 1944, following an announcement that 300 of them were to be sent to a nearby town to clear away rubble—"transfers" were a common ruse for the murder of prisoners—the group, mostly Jews from Greece and Hungary, staged an uprising.[[277]](#cite_note-286) They attacked the SS with stones and hammers, killing three of them, and set crematorium IV on fire with rags soaked in oil that they had hidden.[[278]](#cite_note-287) Hearing the commotion, the *Sonderkommando* at crematorium II believed that a camp uprising had begun and threw their *Oberkapo* into a furnace. After escaping through a fence using wirecutters, they managed to reach [Rajsko](/wiki/Rajsko,_O%C5%9Bwi%C4%99cim_County "Rajsko, Oświęcim County"), where they hid in the granary of an Auschwitz satellite camp, but the SS pursued and killed them by setting the granary on fire.[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreif200544-288)

By the time the rebellion at crematorium IV had been suppressed, 212 members of the *Sonderkommando* were still alive and 451 had been killed.[[280]](#cite_note-289) The dead included [Zalmen Gradowski](/wiki/Zalman_Gradowski "Zalman Gradowski"), who kept notes of his time in Auschwitz and buried them near crematorium III; after the war, another *Sonderkommando* member showed the prosecutors where to dig.[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecki2000a54-290) The notes were published in several formats, including in 2017 as *From the Heart of Hell*.[[282]](#cite_note-291)

### Evacuation and death marches

Further information: [Death marches during the Holocaust](/wiki/Death_marches_during_the_Holocaust "Death marches during the Holocaust")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Auschwitz_Birkenau_Krematorium_IV_-_05.jpg/250px-Auschwitz_Birkenau_Krematorium_IV_-_05.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auschwitz_Birkenau_Krematorium_IV_-_05.jpg)

Ruins of crematorium IV, Auschwitz II, blown up during the revolt

The last mass transports to arrive in Auschwitz were 60,000–70,000 Jews from the [Łódź Ghetto](/wiki/%C5%81%C3%B3d%C5%BA_Ghetto "Łódź Ghetto"), some 2,000 from Theresienstadt, and 8,000 from [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovak_Republic_(1939%E2%80%931945) "Slovak Republic (1939–1945)").[[283]](#cite_note-292) The last selection took place on 30 October 1944.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper1998c174-212) On 1 or 2 November 1944, Heinrich Himmler ordered the SS to halt the mass murder by gas.[[284]](#cite_note-293) On 25 November, he ordered Auschwitz's gas chambers and crematoria be destroyed. The *Sonderkommando* and other prisoners began the job of dismantling the buildings and cleaning up the site.[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b173–174-294) On 18 January 1945, Engelbert Marketsch, a German criminal transferred from [Mauthausen](/wiki/Mauthausen-Gusen_concentration_camp_complex "Mauthausen-Gusen concentration camp complex"), became the last prisoner to be assigned a serial number in Auschwitz, number 202499.[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000227-295)

According to Polish historian Andrzej Strzelecki, the evacuation of the camp was one of its "most tragic chapters".[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecki2000a30-296) Himmler ordered the evacuation of all camps in January 1945, telling camp commanders: "The Führer holds you personally responsible for ... making sure that not a single prisoner from the concentration camps falls alive into the hands of the enemy."[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriedländer2007648-297) The plundered goods from the "Kanada" barracks, together with building supplies, were transported to the German interior. Between 1 December 1944 and 15 January 1945, over one million items of clothing were packed to be shipped out of Auschwitz; 95,000 such parcels were sent to concentration camps in Germany.[[289]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecki2000a41–42-298)

Beginning on 17 January, some 58,000 Auschwitz detainees (about two-thirds Jews)—over 20,000 from Auschwitz I and II and over 30,000 from the subcamps—were evacuated under guard, at first heading west on foot, then by open-topped freight trains, to concentration camps in Germany and Austria: [Bergen-Belsen](/wiki/Bergen-Belsen_concentration_camp "Bergen-Belsen concentration camp"), [Buchenwald](/wiki/Buchenwald_concentration_camp "Buchenwald concentration camp"), [Dachau](/wiki/Dachau_concentration_camp "Dachau concentration camp"), [Flossenburg](/wiki/Flossenb%C3%BCrg_concentration_camp "Flossenbürg concentration camp"), [Gross-Rosen](/wiki/Gross-Rosen_concentration_camp "Gross-Rosen concentration camp"), [Mauthausen](/wiki/Mauthausen-Gusen_concentration_camp_complex "Mauthausen-Gusen concentration camp complex"), [Dora-Mittelbau](/wiki/Mittelbau-Dora_concentration_camp "Mittelbau-Dora concentration camp"), [Ravensbruck](/wiki/Ravensbr%C3%BCck_concentration_camp "Ravensbrück concentration camp"), and [Sachsenhausen](/wiki/Sachsenhausen_concentration_camp "Sachsenhausen concentration camp").[[290]](#cite_note-299) Fewer than 9,000 remained in the camps, deemed too sick to move.[[291]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecki2000a27,_29-300) During the marches, the SS shot or otherwise dispatched anyone unable to continue; "execution details" followed the marchers, killing prisoners who lagged behind.[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecki2000a30-296) [Peter Longerich](/wiki/Peter_Longerich "Peter Longerich") estimated that a quarter of the detainees were thus killed.[[292]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2010415-301) By December 1944 some 15,000 Jewish prisoners had made it from Auschwitz to Bergen-Belsen, where they were liberated by the British on 15 April 1945.[[293]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWachsmann2015335,_597–598-302)

On 20 January, crematoria II and III were blown up, and on 23 January the "Kanada" warehouses were set on fire; they apparently burned for five days. Crematorium IV had been partly demolished after the *Sonderkommando* revolt in October, and the rest of it was destroyed later. On 26 January, one day ahead of the Red Army's arrival, crematorium V was blown up.[[294]](#cite_note-303)

### Liberation

Main article: [Liberation of Auschwitz concentration camp](/wiki/Liberation_of_Auschwitz_concentration_camp "Liberation of Auschwitz concentration camp")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Child_survivors_of_Auschwitz.jpeg/250px-Child_survivors_of_Auschwitz.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Child_survivors_of_Auschwitz.jpeg)

Young survivors at the camp, liberated by the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") in January 1945

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-R69919%2C_KZ_Auschwitz%2C_Brillen.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-R69919%2C_KZ_Auschwitz%2C_Brillen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-R69919,_KZ_Auschwitz,_Brillen.jpg)

Eyeglasses of victims, 1945

The first in the camp complex to be liberated was Auschwitz III, the IG Farben camp at Monowitz; a soldier from the [100th Rifle Division](/wiki/100th_Rifle_Division_(Soviet_Union) "100th Rifle Division (Soviet Union)") of the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") entered the camp around 9 am on Saturday, 27 January 1945.[[295]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000230-304) Other units of the [60th Army](/wiki/60th_Army_(Soviet_Union) "60th Army (Soviet Union)") of the [1st Ukrainian Front](/wiki/1st_Ukrainian_Front "1st Ukrainian Front") arrived in Auschwitz I and II around 3 pm. They found 7,000 prisoners alive in the three main camps, 500 in the other subcamps, and over 600 corpses.[[296]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecki2000a47–48-305) Items found included 837,000 women's garments, 370,000 men's suits, 44,000 pairs of shoes,[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStone201545-306) and 7,000 kg of human hair, estimated by the Soviet war crimes commission to have come from 140,000 people.[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecki2000b409-234) Some of the hair was examined by the Forensic Science Institute in [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków"), where it was found to contain traces of [hydrogen cyanide](/wiki/Hydrogen_cyanide "Hydrogen cyanide"), the main ingredient of [Zyklon B](/wiki/Zyklon_B "Zyklon B").[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecki2000b410-307) [Primo Levi](/wiki/Primo_Levi "Primo Levi") described seeing the first four soldiers on horseback approach Auschwitz III, where he had been in the sick bay. They threw "strangely embarrassed glances at the sprawling bodies, at the battered huts and at us few still alive ...":[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevi2001187-308)

> They did not greet us, nor did they smile; they seemed oppressed not only by compassion but by a confused restraint, which sealed their lips and bound their eyes to the funereal scene. It was that shame we knew so well, the shame that drowned us after the selections, and every time we had to watch, or submit to, some outrage: the shame the Germans did not know, that the just man experiences at another man's crime; the feeling of guilt that such a crime should exist, that it should have been introduced irrevocably into the world of things that exist, and that his will for good should have proved too weak or null, and should not have availed in defence.[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevi2001188-309)

Georgii Elisavetskii, a Soviet soldier who entered one of the barracks, said in 1980 that he could hear other soldiers telling the inmates: "You are free, comrades!" But they did not respond, so he tried in Russian, Polish, German, Ukrainian. Then he used some [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish "Yiddish"): "They think that I am provoking them. They begin to hide. And only when I said to them: 'Do not be afraid, I am a colonel of Soviet Army and a Jew. We have come to liberate you' ... Finally, as if the barrier collapsed ... they rushed toward us shouting, fell on their knees, kissed the flaps of our overcoats, and threw their arms around our legs."[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStone201545-306)

The Soviet military medical service and [Polish Red Cross](/wiki/Polish_Red_Cross "Polish Red Cross") (PCK) set up field hospitals that looked after 4,500 prisoners suffering from the effects of starvation (mostly [diarrhea](/wiki/Diarrhea "Diarrhea")) and [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis"). Local volunteers helped until the Red Cross team arrived from Kraków in early February.[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrzelecki2000a48-310) In Auschwitz II, the layers of excrement on the barracks floors had to be scraped off with shovels. Water was obtained from snow and from fire-fighting wells. Before more help arrived, 2,200 patients there were looked after by a few doctors and 12 PCK nurses. All the patients were later moved to the brick buildings in Auschwitz I, where several blocks became a hospital, with medical personnel working 18-hour shifts.[[302]](#cite_note-311)

The liberation of Auschwitz received little press attention at the time; the Red Army was focusing on its advance toward Germany and liberating the camp had not been one of its key aims. [Boris Polevoi](/wiki/Boris_Polevoy "Boris Polevoy") reported on the liberation in *[Pravda](/wiki/Pravda "Pravda")* on 2 February 1945 but made no mention of Jews;[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStone201546-312) inmates were described collectively as "victims of Fascism".[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERees2005262-313) It was when the Western Allies arrived in [Buchenwald](/wiki/Buchenwald_concentration_camp "Buchenwald concentration camp"), [Bergen-Belsen](/wiki/Bergen-Belsen_concentration_camp "Bergen-Belsen concentration camp"), and [Dachau](/wiki/Dachau_concentration_camp "Dachau concentration camp") in April 1945 that the liberation of the camps received extensive coverage.[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWachsmann201510-314)

## After the war

### Trials of war criminals

Further information: [End of World War II in Europe](/wiki/End_of_World_War_II_in_Europe "End of World War II in Europe"), [Auschwitz trial](/wiki/Auschwitz_trial "Auschwitz trial"), and [Frankfurt Auschwitz trials](/wiki/Frankfurt_Auschwitz_trials "Frankfurt Auschwitz trials")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/AuschwitzGallows2006.JPG/250px-AuschwitzGallows2006.JPG)](/wiki/File:AuschwitzGallows2006.JPG)

Gallows in Auschwitz I where Rudolf Höss was executed on 16 April 1947

Only 789 Auschwitz staff, up to 15 percent, ever stood trial;[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik2000b116,_n. 19-9) most of the cases were pursued in Poland and the [Federal Republic of Germany](/wiki/Federal_Republic_of_Germany "Federal Republic of Germany").[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik2000b108,_113-315) According to [Aleksander Lasik](/wiki/Aleksander_Lasik "Aleksander Lasik"), female SS officers were treated more harshly than male; of the 17 women sentenced, four received the death penalty and the others longer prison terms than the men. He writes that this may have been because there were only 200 women overseers, and therefore they were more visible and memorable to the inmates.[[307]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik2000b110-316)

Camp commandant Rudolf Höss was arrested by the British on 11 March 1946 near [Flensburg](/wiki/Flensburg "Flensburg"), northern Germany, where he had been working as a farmer under the pseudonym Franz Lang. He was imprisoned in [Heide](/wiki/Heide "Heide"), then transferred to [Minden](/wiki/Minden "Minden") for interrogation, part of the [British occupation zone](/wiki/British_occupation_zone "British occupation zone"). From there he was taken to [Nuremberg](/wiki/Nuremberg "Nuremberg") to testify for the defense in the trial of *SS-Obergruppenführer* [Ernst Kaltenbrunner](/wiki/Ernst_Kaltenbrunner "Ernst Kaltenbrunner"). Höss was straightforward about his own role in the mass murder and said he had followed the orders of [Heinrich Himmler](/wiki/Heinrich_Himmler "Heinrich Himmler").[[308]](#cite_note-317)[[j]](#cite_note-319) Extradited to Poland on 25 May 1946,[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik1998b296-318) he wrote his memoirs in custody, first published in Polish in 1951 then in German in 1958 as *Kommandant in Auschwitz*.[[310]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHöss2003Publisher's_Note-320) His trial before the [Supreme National Tribunal](/wiki/Supreme_National_Tribunal "Supreme National Tribunal") in [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw") opened on 11 March 1947; he was sentenced to death on 2 April and hanged in Auschwitz I on 16 April, near crematorium I.[[311]](#cite_note-321)

On 25 November 1947, the [Auschwitz trial](/wiki/Auschwitz_trial "Auschwitz trial") began in [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków"), when Poland's [Supreme National Tribunal](/wiki/Supreme_National_Tribunal "Supreme National Tribunal") brought to court 40 former Auschwitz staff, including commandant [Arthur Liebehenschel](/wiki/Arthur_Liebehenschel "Arthur Liebehenschel"), women's camp leader [Maria Mandel](/wiki/Maria_Mandel "Maria Mandel"), and camp leader [Hans Aumeier](/wiki/Hans_Aumeier "Hans Aumeier"). The trials ended on 22 December 1947, with 23 death sentences, seven life sentences, and nine prison sentences ranging from three to 15 years. [Hans Münch](/wiki/Hans_M%C3%BCnch "Hans Münch"), an SS doctor who had several former prisoners testify on his behalf, was the only person to be acquitted.[[312]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinbacher2005138–139-322)

Other former staff were hanged for war crimes in the [Dachau Trials](/wiki/Dachau_Trials "Dachau Trials") and the [Belsen Trial](/wiki/Belsen_Trial "Belsen Trial"), including camp leaders [Josef Kramer](/wiki/Josef_Kramer "Josef Kramer"), [Franz Hössler](/wiki/Franz_H%C3%B6ssler "Franz Hössler"), and [Vinzenz Schöttl](/wiki/Vinzenz_Sch%C3%B6ttl "Vinzenz Schöttl"); doctor [Friedrich Entress](/wiki/Friedrich_Entress "Friedrich Entress"); and guards [Irma Grese](/wiki/Irma_Grese "Irma Grese") and [Elisabeth Volkenrath](/wiki/Elisabeth_Volkenrath "Elisabeth Volkenrath").[[313]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinbacher2005140-323) [Bruno Tesch](/wiki/Bruno_Tesch "Bruno Tesch") and [Karl Weinbacher](/wiki/Karl_Weinbacher "Karl Weinbacher"), the owner and chief executive officer of the firm [Tesch & Stabenow](/wiki/Tesch_%26_Stabenow "Tesch & Stabenow"), one of the suppliers of Zyklon B, were arrested by the British after the war and executed for knowingly supplying the chemical for use on humans.[[314]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008744-324) The 180-day [Frankfurt Auschwitz trials](/wiki/Frankfurt_Auschwitz_trials "Frankfurt Auschwitz trials"), held in [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") from 20 December 1963 to 20 August 1965, tried 22 defendants, including two dentists, a doctor, two camp adjudants and the camp's pharmacist. The 700-page indictment, presenting the testimony of 254 witnesses, was accompanied by a 300-page report about the camp, *Nationalsozialistische Konzentrationslager*, written by historians from the *[Institut für Zeitgeschichte](/wiki/Institute_of_Contemporary_History_(Munich) "Institute of Contemporary History (Munich)")* in Germany, including [Martin Broszat](/wiki/Martin_Broszat "Martin Broszat") and [Helmut Krausnick](/wiki/Helmut_Krausnick "Helmut Krausnick"). The report became the basis of their book, *Anatomy of the SS State* (1968), the first comprehensive study of the camp and the SS. The court convicted 19 of the defendants, giving six of them life sentences and the others between three and ten years.[[315]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWittmann20053-325) [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") also held trials against several former staff members of Auschwitz. One of the defendants they tried was [Horst Fischer](/wiki/Horst_Fischer "Horst Fischer"). Fischer, one of the highest-ranking SS physicians in the camp, had personally selected at least 75,000 men, women, and children to be gassed. He was arrested in 1965. The following year, he was convicted of crimes against humanity, sentenced to death, and executed by [guillotine](/wiki/Guillotine "Guillotine"). Fischer was the highest-ranking SS physician from Auschwitz to ever be tried by a German court.[[316]](#cite_note-326)

### Legacy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/2_Birkenau_3.JPG/250px-2_Birkenau_3.JPG)](/wiki/File:2_Birkenau_3.JPG)

Barracks at Auschwitz II

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1992-101-026A%2C_KZ_Auschwitz%2C_Einfahrt.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1992-101-026A%2C_KZ_Auschwitz%2C_Einfahrt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1992-101-026A,_KZ_Auschwitz,_Einfahrt.jpg)

Auschwitz II gate in 1959

In the decades since its liberation, Auschwitz has become a primary symbol of the Holocaust. [Seweryna Szmaglewska](/wiki/Seweryna_Szmaglewska "Seweryna Szmaglewska")'s 1945 autobiography *[Dymy nad Birkenau](/wiki/Dymy_nad_Birkenau "Dymy nad Birkenau")* (*Smoke over Birkenau*) has been credited with spreading knowledge about the camp to the general public.[[317]](#cite_note-Huener2007-327): 167[[318]](#cite_note-:1-328) Historian [Timothy D. Snyder](/wiki/Timothy_D._Snyder "Timothy D. Snyder") attributes this to the camp's high death toll and "unusual combination of an industrial camp complex and a killing facility", which left behind far more witnesses than single-purpose killing facilities such as [Chełmno](/wiki/Che%C5%82mno_extermination_camp "Chełmno extermination camp") or [Treblinka](/wiki/Treblinka_extermination_camp "Treblinka extermination camp").[[319]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnyder2010382–383-329) In 2005 the [United Nations General Assembly](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly "United Nations General Assembly") designated 27 January, the date of the camp's liberation, as [International Holocaust Remembrance Day](/wiki/International_Holocaust_Remembrance_Day "International Holocaust Remembrance Day").[[320]](#cite_note-330) [Helmut Schmidt](/wiki/Helmut_Schmidt "Helmut Schmidt") visited the site in November 1977, the first [West German](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") [chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany_(1949%E2%80%93present) "Chancellor of Germany (1949–present)") to do so, followed by his successor, [Helmut Kohl](/wiki/Helmut_Kohl "Helmut Kohl"), in November 1989.[[321]](#cite_note-331) In a statement on the 50th anniversary of the liberation, Kohl said that "[t]he darkest and most awful chapter in German history was written at Auschwitz."[[322]](#cite_note-332) In January 2020, world leaders gathered at [Yad Vashem](/wiki/Yad_Vashem "Yad Vashem") in Jerusalem to commemorate the 75th anniversary.[[323]](#cite_note-333) It was the city's largest-ever political gathering, with over 45 heads of state and world leaders, including royalty.[[324]](#cite_note-334) At Auschwitz itself, [Reuven Rivlin](/wiki/Reuven_Rivlin "Reuven Rivlin") and [Andrzej Duda](/wiki/Andrzej_Duda "Andrzej Duda"), the presidents of Israel and Poland, laid [wreaths](/wiki/Wreath "Wreath").[[325]](#cite_note-335)

Notable memoirists of the camp include [Primo Levi](/wiki/Primo_Levi "Primo Levi"), [Elie Wiesel](/wiki/Elie_Wiesel "Elie Wiesel"), and [Tadeusz Borowski](/wiki/Tadeusz_Borowski "Tadeusz Borowski").[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnyder2010383-251) Levi's *[If This is a Man](/wiki/If_This_is_a_Man "If This is a Man")*, first published in Italy in 1947 as *Se questo è un uomo*, became a classic of Holocaust literature, an "imperishable masterpiece".[[326]](#cite_note-336)[[k]](#cite_note-338) Wiesel wrote about his imprisonment at Auschwitz in *[Night](/wiki/Night_(memoir) "Night (memoir)")* (1960) and other works, and became a prominent spokesman against ethnic violence; in 1986, he was awarded the [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize").[[328]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorwegian_Nobel_Committee1986-339) Camp survivor [Simone Veil](/wiki/Simone_Veil "Simone Veil") was elected President of the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament"), serving from 1979 to 1982.[[329]](#cite_note-340) Two Auschwitz victims—[Maximilian Kolbe](/wiki/Maximilian_Kolbe "Maximilian Kolbe"), a priest who volunteered to die by starvation in place of a stranger, and [Edith Stein](/wiki/Edith_Stein "Edith Stein"), a Jewish convert to Catholicism—were named saints of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").[[330]](#cite_note-341)

In 2017, a [Körber Foundation](/wiki/K%C3%B6rber_Foundation "Körber Foundation") survey found that 40 percent of 14-year-olds in Germany did not know what Auschwitz was.[[331]](#cite_note-342)[[332]](#cite_note-343) The following year a survey organized by the [Claims Conference](/wiki/Claims_Conference "Claims Conference"), [United States Holocaust Memorial Museum](/wiki/United_States_Holocaust_Memorial_Museum "United States Holocaust Memorial Museum") and others found that 41 percent of 1,350 American adults surveyed, and 66 percent of [millennials](/wiki/Millennials "Millennials"), did not know what Auschwitz was, while 22 percent said they had never heard of the Holocaust.[[333]](#cite_note-344)[[334]](#cite_note-345) A [CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN")-[ComRes](/wiki/ComRes "ComRes") poll in 2018 found a similar situation in Europe.[[335]](#cite_note-346)

### Auschwitz-Birkenau State Museum

Main article: [Auschwitz-Birkenau State Museum](/wiki/Auschwitz-Birkenau_State_Museum "Auschwitz-Birkenau State Museum")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Czeslawa_Kwoka_-_Brasse.jpg/250px-Czeslawa_Kwoka_-_Brasse.jpg)](/wiki/File:Czeslawa_Kwoka_-_Brasse.jpg)

[Czesława Kwoka](/wiki/Czes%C5%82awa_Kwoka "Czesława Kwoka"), photographed in Auschwitz by [Wilhelm Brasse](/wiki/Wilhelm_Brasse "Wilhelm Brasse") of the camp's [Erkennungsdienst](/wiki/Auschwitz_Erkennungsdienst "Auschwitz Erkennungsdienst")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Israeli_Air_Force_jets_Fly-over_Auschwitz_concentration_camp.jpg/250px-Israeli_Air_Force_jets_Fly-over_Auschwitz_concentration_camp.jpg)](/wiki/File:Israeli_Air_Force_jets_Fly-over_Auschwitz_concentration_camp.jpg)

[Israeli Air Force](/wiki/Israeli_Air_Force "Israeli Air Force") [F-15 Eagles](/wiki/F-15_Eagle "F-15 Eagle") fly over Auschwitz II-Birkenau, 2003

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/End_of_the_railway_line%2C_Auschwitz-Birkenau%2C_2012_%282%29.jpg/250px-End_of_the_railway_line%2C_Auschwitz-Birkenau%2C_2012_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:End_of_the_railway_line,_Auschwitz-Birkenau,_2012_(2).jpg)

End of the rail track inside Auschwitz II

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Mattarella_Auschwitz.jpg/250px-Mattarella_Auschwitz.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mattarella_Auschwitz.jpg)

Italian president [Sergio Mattarella](/wiki/Sergio_Mattarella "Sergio Mattarella") standing in front of the "Death Wall"

On 2 July 1947, the Polish government passed a law establishing a state memorial to remember "the martyrdom of the Polish nation and other nations in Oswiecim".[[336]](#cite_note-347) The museum established its exhibits at Auschwitz I; after the war, the barracks in Auschwitz II-Birkenau had been mostly dismantled and moved to Warsaw to be used on building sites. Dwork and van Pelt write that, in addition, Auschwitz I played a more central role in the persecution of the Polish people, in opposition to the importance of Auschwitz II to the Jews, including Polish Jews.[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDworkvan_Pelt2002364ff-348) An exhibition opened in Auschwitz I in 1955, displaying prisoner [mug shots](/wiki/Mug_shot "Mug shot"); hair, suitcases, and shoes taken from murdered prisoners; canisters of Zyklon B pellets; and other objects related to the killings.[[338]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPermanent_exhibition_–_Auschwitz_I-349) [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") added the camp to its list of [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") in 1979.[[339]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUNESCO,_''World_Heritage_List''-350) All the museum's directors were, until 1990, former Auschwitz prisoners. Visitors to the site have increased from 492,500 in 2001, to over one million in 2009,[[340]](#cite_note-351) to two million in 2016.[[341]](#cite_note-352)

There have been protracted disputes over the perceived Christianization of the site. Pope [John Paul II](/wiki/John_Paul_II "John Paul II") celebrated [mass](/wiki/Mass_(Roman_Rite) "Mass (Roman Rite)") over the train tracks leading to Auschwitz II-Birkenau on 7 June 1979[[342]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarroll2002-353) and called the camp "the [Golgotha](/wiki/Calvary "Calvary") of our age", referring to the [crucifixion of Jesus](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus").[[343]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBerger2017165-354) More controversy followed when [Carmelite](/wiki/Carmelite "Carmelite") nuns founded a convent in 1984 in a former theater outside the camp's perimeter, near block 11 of Auschwitz I,[[344]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDworkvan_Pelt2002369–370-355) after which a local priest and some survivors [erected a large cross](/wiki/Auschwitz_cross "Auschwitz cross")—one that had been used during the pope's mass—behind block 11 to commemorate 152 Polish inmates shot by the Germans in 1941.[[345]](#cite_note-356)[[346]](#cite_note-357) After a long dispute, Pope John Paul II intervened and the nuns moved the convent elsewhere in 1993.[[347]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBerger2017166-358) The cross remained, triggering the "War of the Crosses", as more crosses were erected to commemorate Christian victims, despite international objections. The Polish government and Catholic Church eventually agreed to remove all but the original.[[348]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBerger2017167-359)

On 4 September 2003, despite a protest from the museum, three [Israeli Air Force](/wiki/Israeli_Air_Force "Israeli Air Force") [F-15 Eagles](/wiki/F-15_Eagle "F-15 Eagle") [performed a fly-over](/wiki/Israeli_Air_Force_flight_over_Auschwitz "Israeli Air Force flight over Auschwitz") of Auschwitz II-Birkenau during a ceremony at the camp below. All three pilots were descendants of Holocaust survivors, including the man who led the flight, Major-General [Amir Eshel](/wiki/Amir_Eshel "Amir Eshel").[[349]](#cite_note-360) On 27 January 2015, some 300 Auschwitz survivors gathered with world leaders under a giant tent at the entrance to Auschwitz II to commemorate the 70th anniversary of the camp's liberation.[[350]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBBC_News2015a-361)[[l]](#cite_note-363)

Museum curators consider visitors who pick up items from the ground to be thieves, and local police will charge them as such; the maximum penalty is a 10-year prison sentence.[[352]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBBC2016-364) In 2017 two British youths from the [Perse School](/wiki/The_Perse_School "The Perse School") were fined in Poland after picking up buttons and shards of decorative glass in 2015 from the "Kanada" area of Auschwitz II, where camp victims' personal effects were stored.[[353]](#cite_note-365) The 16 ft (4.9 m) *Arbeit Macht Frei* sign over the main camp's gate was stolen in December 2009 by a Swedish former neo-Nazi and two Polish men. The sign was later recovered.[[354]](#cite_note-366)

In 2018 the Polish government passed an amendment to its [Act on the Institute of National Remembrance](/wiki/Act_on_the_Institute_of_National_Remembrance "Act on the Institute of National Remembrance"), making it a criminal offence to violate the "good name" of Poland by accusing it of crimes committed by Germany in the Holocaust, which would include referring to Auschwitz and other camps as ["Polish death camps"](/wiki/%22Polish_death_camp%22_controversy "\"Polish death camp\" controversy").[[355]](#cite_note-367) Staff at the museum were accused by nationalist media in Poland of focusing too much on the fate of the Jews in Auschwitz at the expense of ethnic Poles. The brother of the museum's director, [Piotr Cywiński](/wiki/Piotr_Cywi%C5%84ski "Piotr Cywiński"), wrote that Cywiński had experienced "50 days of incessant hatred".[[356]](#cite_note-368) After discussions with Israel's prime minister, amid international concern that the new law would stifle research, the Polish government adjusted the amendment so that anyone accusing Poland of complicity would be guilty only of a civil offence.[[357]](#cite_note-369)

Similar legislation deprived Jewish members of other professions of the right to practise.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich201038–39-15)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-33)** [Danuta Czech](/wiki/Danuta_Czech "Danuta Czech") (*[Auschwitz 1940–1945](/wiki/Auschwitz_1940%E2%80%931945 "Auschwitz 1940–1945")*, Volume V, [Auschwitz-Birkenau State Museum](/wiki/Auschwitz-Birkenau_State_Museum "Auschwitz-Birkenau State Museum"), 2000): "June 14 [1940]: The first transport of Polish political prisoners arrived from the Tarnów prison: 728 men sent to Auschwitz by the commander of the Sipo u. SD (Security Police and Security Service) in Cracow. These prisoners were given camp serial numbers 31 to 758. The transport included many healthy young men fit for military service, who had been caught trying to cross the Polish southern border in order to make their way to the Polish Armed Forces being formed in France. The organizers of this illegal emigration operation were also in this transport, along with resistance organizers, political and community activists, members of the Polish intelligentsia, Catholic priests, and Jews, arrested in the 'AB' (Außerordentliche Befriedungsaktion) operation organized by [Hans Frank](/wiki/Hans_Frank "Hans Frank") in the spring of 1940. At the same time, a further 100 SS men—officers and SS enlisted men—were sent to reinforce the camp garrison."[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000121–122-32)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-42)** [Franciszek Piper](/wiki/Franciszek_Piper "Franciszek Piper") writes that, according to post-war testimony from several inmates, as well as from Rudolf Höss (Auschwitz commandant from May 1940), the gas chamber at Auschwitz I could hold 1,000 people.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiper2000b128-39)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-47)** [Danuta Czech](/wiki/Danuta_Czech "Danuta Czech") (*[Auschwitz 1940–1945](/wiki/Auschwitz_1940%E2%80%931945 "Auschwitz 1940–1945")*, Volume V, [Auschwitz-Birkenau State Museum](/wiki/Auschwitz-Birkenau_State_Museum "Auschwitz-Birkenau State Museum"), 2000): "February 15, 1942: "The first transport of Jews arrested by the Stapo (State Police) in Katowice and fated to die at Auschwitz arrived from Beuthen. There, they were killed with the use of Zyklon B gas."[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000142-46)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-50)** [Mary Fulbrook](/wiki/Mary_Fulbrook "Mary Fulbrook") (*A Small Town Near Auschwitz: Ordinary Nazis and the Holocaust*, Oxford University Press, 2012): "Gunter Faerber, for example, recalled the moment in February 1942 when the Jews of Beuthen (Bytom in Polish), where his grandmother lived, were brought through Bedzin on their way to Auschwitz. ... This was the first registered transport sent to Auschwitz by RSHA IV B4 (the Jewish Office, directed by SS-Obersturmbannführer Adolf Eichmann)."[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECzech2000144-55)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-158)** This was the third set of serial numbers started in the camp.[[123]](#cite_note-serialUSHHM-129)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-244)** [Robert Jan van Pelt](/wiki/Robert_Jan_van_Pelt "Robert Jan van Pelt") (*The Case for Auschwitz*, 2002): "This figure [1.1 million] has been endorsed by all serious, professional historians who have studied the complex history of Auschwitz in some detail, by the Holocaust research institute at Yad Vashem in Jerusalem, and by the United States Holocaust Memorial Museum in Washington, D.C."[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_Pelt2016109-241)

   Earlier estimates included [Raul Hilberg](/wiki/Raul_Hilberg "Raul Hilberg")'s 1961 work, *[The Destruction of the European Jews](/wiki/The_Destruction_of_the_European_Jews "The Destruction of the European Jews")*, which estimated that up to one million Jews had died in the camp.[[235]](#cite_note-242) In 1983 French scholar George Wellers was one of the first to use German data on deportations to calculate the death toll; he arrived at a figure of 1,471,595 deaths, including 1.35 million Jews and 86,675 non-Jewish Poles.[[236]](#cite_note-243)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-272)** The escapees included 396 Polish men and 10 Polish women; 164 men from the Soviet Union (including 50 prisoners of war), and 15 women; 112 Jewish men and three Jewish women; 36 Romani/Sinti men and two women; 22 German men and nine women; 19 Czech men and four women; two Austrian men; one Yugoslav woman and one man; and 15 other men and one woman.[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEŚwiebocki2000233-271)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-319)** In his testimony, according to Polish historian [Aleksander Lasik](/wiki/Aleksander_Lasik "Aleksander Lasik"), "Höss neither protected anyone nor evaded his own responsibility. His stance came as a surprise to many, especially those who viewed him as a bloodthirsty beast. Instead, he viewed his crimes in terms of the technical obstacles and challenges with which he had to cope. Höss stated that he led the killings in Auschwitz on express orders of Reichsführer Himmler."[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELasik1998b296-318)
11. **[^](#cite_ref-338)** In *[The Drowned and the Saved](/wiki/The_Drowned_and_the_Saved "The Drowned and the Saved")* (1986), Levi wrote that the concentration camps represented the epitome of the totalitarian system: "[N]ever has there existed a state that was really "totalitarian" ... Never has some form of reaction, a corrective of the total tyranny, been lacking, not even in the Third Reich or Stalin's Soviet Union: in both cases, public opinion, the magistrature, the foreign press, the churches, the feeling for justice and humanity that ten or twenty years of tyranny were not enough to eradicate, have to a greater or lesser extent acted as a brake. Only in the Lager [camp] was the restraint from below nonexistent, and the power of these small [satraps](/wiki/Satrap "Satrap") absolute."[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevi201735–36-337)
12. **[^](#cite_ref-363)** Attendees included the president of the [World Jewish Congress](/wiki/World_Jewish_Congress "World Jewish Congress"), [Ronald Lauder](/wiki/Ronald_Lauder "Ronald Lauder"), Polish president [Bronisław Komorowski](/wiki/Bronis%C5%82aw_Komorowski "Bronisław Komorowski"), French President [François Hollande](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Hollande "François Hollande"), German President [Joachim Gauck](/wiki/Joachim_Gauck "Joachim Gauck"), the film director [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg"), and King [Willem-Alexander of the Netherlands](/wiki/Willem-Alexander_of_the_Netherlands "Willem-Alexander of the Netherlands").[[350]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBBC_News2015a-361)[[351]](#cite_note-362)
